A ponderous mass; something heavy. A man leaps better with weights in his hands.
Pressure; burden; as the weight of grief; weight of care; weight of business; weight of government.
weightily: With force or impressiveness; with moral power.
weightiness: Solidity; force; impressiveness; power of convincing; as the weightiness of an argument.
weighty: Having great weight; heavy; ponderous; as a weighty body.
Important; forcible; momentous; adapted to turn the balance in the mind, or to convince; as weighty reasons; weighty matters; weighty considerations or arguments.
Rigorous; severe; as our weightier judgment.
welaway: an exclamation expressive of grief or sorrow, equivalent to alas. It is a compound of Sax. Wa, wo and laThe original is wa-la, which is doubtless the origin of our common exclamation, O la, and to this, wa, wo, is added. The true orthography would be wa la wa. But the word is, I believe, wholly obsolete.
welcome: Received with gladness; admitted willingly to the house, entertainment and company; as a welcome guest.
Grateful; pleasing; as a welcome present; welcome news.
Free to have or enjoy gratuitously. You are welcome to the use of my library. To bid welcome, to receive with professions of kindness.
is used elliptically for you are welcome. Welcome, great monarch, to your own. Welcome to our house, an herb.
Salutation of a new comer. Welcome ever smiles-- .
welcomer: One who salutes or receives kindly a new comer.
welcoming: Saluting or receiving with kindness a new comer or guest.
weld: that in which it was inserted. This is now called grafting by approach or inarching. [See Graft.I ABLACTATION.
A plant of the genus Reseda, used by dyers to give a yellow color, and sometimes called dyers weed. It is much cultivated in Kent for the London dyers.
To unite or hammer into firm union, as two pieces of iron, when heated almost to fusion.
wold: A plant of the genus Reseda, used by dyers to give a yellow color, and sometimes called dyers weed. It is much cultivated in Kent for the London dyers.
in Saxon, is the same as wald and weald, a wood, sometimes perhaps a lawn or plain. Wald signifies also power, dominion, from waldan, to rule. These words occur in names.
welded: Forged or beat into union in an intense heat.
welding-heat: The heat necessary for welding iron bars, which is said to be degrees by Wedgwood’s pyrometer, and 8877 degrees by Fahrenheit.
welfare: Exemption from misfortune, sickness, calamity or evil; the enjoyment of health and the common blessings of life; prosperity; happiness; applied to persons.
Exemption from any unusual evil or calamity; the enjoyment of peace and prosperity, or the ordinary blessings of society and civil government; applied to states.
welk: To decline; to fade; to decay; to fall. When ruddy Phoebus gins to welk in west.
To contract; to shorten. Now sad winter welked hath the day-- .
welked: or a. Contracted into wrinkles or ridges. --Horns welkd and wavd like the enridged sea.
welkin: The visible regions of the air; the vault of heaven. Welkin eye, in Shakespeare, is interpreted by Johnson, a blue eye, from welkin, the sky; by Todd, a rolling eye, from Sax. Wealcan, to roll; and by Entick, a languishing eye. See Ielk. It is obsolete, at least in New England.
well: A spring; a fountain; the issuing of water from the earth. Begin then, sisters of the sacred well.
A pit or cylindrical hole, sunk perpendicularly into the earth to such a depth as to reach a supply of water, and walled with stone to prevent the earth from caving in.
In ships, an apartment in the middle of a ships hold, to inclose the pumps, from the bottom to the lower deck.

In a fishing vessel, an apartment in the middle of the hold, made tight at the sides, but having holes perforated int he bottom to let in fresh water for the preservation of fish, while they are transported to market.
In the military art, a hole or excavation in the earth, in mining, from which run branches or galleries.
well-drain: A drain or vent for water, somewhat like a well or pit, Me1S serving to discharge the water of wet land.
To drain land by means of wells or pits, which receive the water, and from which it is discharged by machinery.
well-hole: In architecture, the hole or space left in a floor for the stairs.
well-room: In a boat, a place in the bottom where the water is collected, and whence it is thrown out with a scoop.
well-spring: A source of continual supply. Proverbs 16:22.
well-water: The water that flows into a well from subterraneous springs; water drawn from a well.
welladay: alas, Johnson supposes to be a corruption of welaway, which see.
wellbeing: Welfare; happiness; prosperity; as, virtue is essential to the well being of men or of society.
well-born: Born of a noble or respectable family; not of mean birth.
well-done: A word of praise; bravely; nobly; in a right manner.
well-favored: Handsome; well formed; beautiful; pleasing to the eye. Genesis 29:27.
well-grounded: Well founded; having a solid foundation.
well-met: A term of salutation denoting joy at meeting.
well-minded: Well disposed; having a good mind.
well-spent: Spent or passed in virtue; as a well-spent life; well- spent days.
well-spoken: Speaking well; speaking with fitness or grace; or speaking kindly.
Spoken with propriety; as well-spoken words.
well-wisher: One who wishes the good of another.
welsh: The language of Wales or of the Welsh.

The general name of the inhabitants of Wales. The word signifies foreigners or wanderers, and was given to this people by other nations, probably because they came from some distant country. The Welsh call themselves Cymry, in the plural, and a Welshman Cymro, and their country Cymra, of which the adjective is Cymreig, and the name of their language, Cymraeg. They are supposed to be the Cimbri of Jutland.
welt: A border; a kind of hem or edging, as on a garment or piece of cloth, or on a shoe.
To furnish with a welt; to sew on a border.
welter: To roll, as the body of an animal; but usually, to roll or wallow in some foul matter; as, to welter in blood or in filth.
weltering: Rolling; wallowing; as in mire, blood, or other filthy matter.
wen: An encysted swelling or tumor; also, a fleshy excrescence growing on animals, sometimes to a large size.
wench: A young woman of ill fame.
In America, a black or colored female servant; a negress.
To frequent the company of women of ill fame.
wend: To go; to pass to or from.
wennish: Having the nature of a wen.
wenny: Having the nature of a wen.
went: of the obsolete verb wend. We now arrange went in grammar as the preterit of go, but in origin it has no connection with it.
wept: and pp. of weep. When he had come near, he beheld the city and wept over it. Luke 19:41.
were: er, which when prolonged, becomes ware. This is used as the imperfect tense plural of be; we were, you were, they were; and in some other tenses. It is the Danish verb vaerer, to be, to exist, and in origin has no connection with be, nor with was. It is united with be, to supply its want of tenses, as went is with go.
weregild: Formerly, the price of a man’s head; a compensation paid for a man killed, partly to the king for the loss of a subject, and partly tot he lord of the vassal, and partly to the next of kin. It was paid by the murderer.
wernerian: a2. Pertaining to Werner, the German mineralogist, who arranged minerals in classesaccording to their external characters.
wernerite: A mineral, regarded by Werener as a subspecies of scapolite; called foliated scapolite. It is named from that distinguished mineralogist, Werner. It is found massive, and crystalized in octahedral prisms with four sided pyramidical terminations, disseminated in rocks or grayish or red feldspar. It is imperfectly lamellar, of a greenish, grayish, or olive green color, with a pearly or resinous luster. It is softer than feldspar, and melts into a white enamel.
wert: the second person singular of the subjunctive imperfect tense of be. Werth, worth, in names, signifies a farm, court or village.
west: In strictness, that point of the horizon where the sun sets at the equinox, or any point in a direct line between the spectator or other object, and that point of the horizon; or west is the intersection of the prime vertical with the horizon, on that side where the sun sets. West is directly opposite to east, and one of the cardinal points. In a less strict sense, west is the region of the hemisphere near the point where the sun sets when in the equator. Thus we say, a star sets in the west, a meteor appears in the west, a cloud rises in the west.

A country situated in the region towards the sun-setting, with respect to another. Thus in the United States, the inhabitants of the Atlantic states speak of the inhabitants of Ohio, Kentucky or Missouri, and call them people of the west; and formerly, the empire of Rome was called the empire of the West, in opposition to the empire of the East, the seat of which was Constantinople.

Being in a line towards the point where the sun sets when in the equator; or in a looser sense, being in the region near the line of direction towards that point, either on the earth or in the heavens. This shall be your west border. Numbers 34:6.
Coming or moving from the west or western region; as a west wind.
To the western region; at the westward; more westward; as, Ireland lies west of England.
westerly: Being towards the west; situated in the western region; as the westerly parts of England.
Moving from the westward; as a westerly wind.
Tending, going or moving towards the west; as a man traveling westerly.
western: Being in the west, or int he region nearly in the direction of west; being in that quarter where the sun sets; as the western shore of France; the western ocean.
Moving in a line to the part where the sun sets; as, the ship makes a western course.
westing: Space or distance westward; or departure; as the westing and southing of a ship.
westward: Towards the west; as, to ride or sail westward.
westwardly: In a direction towards the west; as, to pass westwardly.
wet: Containing water, as wet land, or a wet cloth; or having water or other liquid upon the surface, as a wet table. Wet implies more water or liquid than moist or humid.
Rainy; as wet weather; a wet season.
Water or wetness; moisture or humidity in considerable degree. Wear thick shoes or pattens to keep your feet from the wet.
Rainy weather; foggy or misty weather.
pret. and pp. wet. But wetted is sometimes used.
wetness: The state of being wet, either by being soaked or filled with liquor, or by having a liquid adherent to the surface; as the wetness of land; the wetness of a cloth. It implies more water or liquid than humidness or moisture.
A watery or moist state of the atmosphere; a state of being rainy, foggy or misty; as the wetness of weather or the season.
wettish: Somewhat wet; moist; humid. WExXor i. To grow; to wax.
whack: To strike. This is probably the primary word on which is formed thwack. Whack is a vulgar word.
whale: The general name of an order of animals inhabiting the ocean, arranged in zoology under the name of Cete or Cetacea, and belonging to the class Mammalia in the Linnean system. The common whale is of the genus Balaena. It is the largest animal of which we have any account, and probably the largest in the world. It is sometimes ninety feet in length in the northern seas, and in the torrid zone much larger. The whale furnishes us with oil, whalebone
whalebone: A firm elastic substance taken from the upper jaw of the whale, used as a stiffening in stays, fans, screens
whale-fishery: The fishery or occupation of taking whales.
whame: A species of fly, tabanus, the burrel fly, that annoys horses.
whapper: Something uncommonly large of the kind. So thumper is connected with thump, to strike with a heavy blow.
wharf: A perpendicular bank or mound or timber or stone and earth, raised on the shore of a harbor, or extending some distance into the water, for the convenience of lading and unlading ships and other vessels. This name is also given to the wider part of a canal, where boats lie while loading and unloading. The two longest wharfs in New England are at Boston and at New Haven. The latter is much the longest, extending into the harbor about three quarter of a mile.
To guard or secure by a wharf or firm wall of timber or stone; as, the western bank of the Connecticut is wharfed at Hartford, to prevent the river from wearing away the land.
wharfage: The fee or duty paid for the privilege of using a wharf for loading or unloading goods, timber, wood
wharfinger: A man who has the care of a wharf, or the proprietor of a wharf.
what: That which. Say what you will, is the same as say that which you will.
Which part. Consider what is due to nature, and what to art or labor.
What is the substitute for a sentence or clause of a sentence. I tell thee what, corporal, I could tear her. Here what relates to the last clause, I could tear her; this is what I tell yous .

What is used as an adjective, of both genders, often in specifying sorts or particulars. See what colors this silk exhibits. I know what qualities you desire in a friend; that is, I know the qualities which you desire.
whatever: Being this or that; being of one nature or another; being one thing or another; any thing that may be. Whatever is read, let it be read with attention. Whatever measure may be adopted, let it be with due caution. Whatever you do, let it be done with prudence.
All that; the whole that; all particulars that. At once came forth whatever creeps.
whatsoever: a compound of what, so, and ever, has the sense of whatever, and is less used than the latter. Indeed it is nearly obsolete. Whatso, in a like sense, is entirely obsolete.
wheat: A plant of the genus Triticum, and the seed of the plant, which furnishes a white flour for bread, and next to rice, is the grain most generally used by the human race. Of this grain the varieties are numerous, as red wheat, white wheat, bald wheat, bearded wheat, winter wheat, summer wheat
wheat-bird: A bird that feeds on wheat.
wheat-ear: The English name of the Motacilla aenanthe; called also white-tail and fallow-finch.
wheaten: Hweetn. Made of wheat; as wheaten bread.
wheedle: To flatter; to entice by soft words. To learn th unlucky art of wheeling fools.
wheedling: The act of flattering or enticing.
wheel: A circular frame of wood, iron or other metal, consisting of a nave or hub, into which are inserted spokes which sustain a rim or felly; the whole turning on an axis. The name is also given to a solid circular or round piece of wood or metal, which revolves on an axis. The wheel and axle constitute one of the mechanical powers.
A carriage that moves on wheels.
An instrument for torturing criminals; as an examination made by the rack and the wheel.
A machine for spinning thread, of various kinds.
wheel-animal: A genus of animalcules, with arms for taking their prey, resembling wheels.
wheel-barrow: A barrow moved on a single wheel.
wheel-boat: A boat with wheels, to be used either on water or upon inclined planes or rail-ways.
wheel-fire: In chemistry, a fire which encompasses the crucible without touching it.
wheel-shaped: In botany, rotate; monopetalous, expanding into a flat border at top, with scarcely any tube; as a wheel-shaped corol.
wheel-wright: A man whose occupation is to make wheels and wheel-carriages, as carts and wagons.
wheeled: Conveyed on wheels; turned; rolled round.
wheeling: Conveying on wheels or in a wheel-carriage; turning.
The act of conveying on wheels.
The act of passing on wheels, or convenience for passing on wheels. We say, it is good wheeling, or bad wheeling, according to the state of the roads.
A turning or circular movement of troops embodied.
wheeze: To breathe hard and with an audible sound, as persons affected with asthma.
whelk: A wrinkle; inequality on the surface; protuberance; a pustule. [See Welk and Weal.I .
A shell of the genus Bussinum, or trumpetshell, univalvular, spiral and gibbous, with an oval aperture ending in a short canal or gutter.
whelm: To cover with water or other fluid; to cover by immersion in something that envelops on all sides; as, to whelm a person or a company in the seas; to whelm a caravan in sand or dust.
To cover completely; to immerse deeply; to overburden; as, to whelm one in sorrows.
To throw over so as to cover. WHELIM.
whelmed: Covered, as by being plunged or immersed.
whelp: The young of the canine species, and of several other beasts of prey; a puppy; a cub; as a bear robbed of her whelps; lions whelps.
To bring forth young, as the female of the canine species and some other beasts of prey.
when: At the time. We were present when General LaFayette embarked at Havre for New York.
At what time. When shall these things be? Matthew 24:3.
Which time. I was adopted heir by his consent; since when, his oath is broke.
After the time that. When the act is passed, the public will be satisfied.
At what time. Kings may take their advantage, when and how they list. When as, at the time when; what time. When as sacred light began to dawn.
whence: From what place. Whence and what art thou?
From what source. Whence shall we derive hope? Whence comes this honor? Whence hath this man this wisdom? Maithew 13:54.
From which premises, principles or facts. These facts or principles are admitted, whence it follows, that judgment must be entered for the plaintiff.
How; by what way or means. Mark 12:37.

In general, from which person, cause, place, principle or circumstance. From whence may be considered as tautological, from being implied in whence; but the use is well authorized, and in some cases the use of it seems to give force or beauty to the phrase. We ascended the mountain, from whence we took a view of the beautiful plains below. Of whence is not now used.
whencesoever: From what place soever; from what cause or source soever. Any idea, whencesoever we have it-- .
whenever: At whatever time. Whenever you come, you will be kindly received.
whensoever: At what time soever; at whatever time.
where: At which place or places. She visited the place where first she was so happy-- . In all places where I record my name, I will come to thee and I will bless thee. Exodus 20:24.
At or in what place. Adam, where art thou? Genesis 3:9.
At the place in which. Where I though the remnant of my age should have been cherishd by her child-like duty: .
Whither; to what place, or from what place. Where are you going? Where are you from? Any where, in any place. I sought the man, but could not find him any where.
whereabout: Near what place. Whereabout did you meet your friend?
Concerning which. The object whereabout they are — conversant.
whereas: When in fact or truth, implying opposition to something that precedes. Are not those found to be the greatest zealots, who are most notoriously ignorant? Whereas true zeal should always begin with true knowledge.

The thing being so that; considering that things are so; implying an admission of facts, sometimes followed by a different statement, and sometimes by inferences or something consequent, as in the law style, where a preamble introduces a law. Whereas wars are generally causes of poverty-- .
whereat: At which. Whereat he was no less angry and ashamed, than desirous to obey Zelmane.
whereby: By which. You take my life, when you do take the means whereby I live.
By what. Whereby shall I know this? Luke 1:78.
wherefore: For which reason. Wherefore by their fruits ye shall know them. Matthew 7:20.
Why; for what reason. Wherefore didst thou doubt? Matthew 14:31.
wherein: In which; in which thing, time, respect, bookThis is the thing wherein you have erred.
In what. Yet ye say, wherein have we wearied him? Malachi 2:17.
whereness: Ubiety; imperfect locality. A point hath no dimensions, but only a whereness, and is next to nothing.
whereof: Of which. We are not guilty of the crime whereof we are accused.
Of what. Whereof was this house built? How this world, when and whereof created-- .
whereon: On which; as the ground whereon we tread.z
On what. Whereon do we stand?
wheresoever: In what place soever; in whatever place, or in any place indefinitely. Seize the thief, wheresoever he may be found.
wherethrough: through which, is not in use.
whereto: To which. Whereto we have already attained-- Philippians 3:16.
whereupon: Upon which. The townsmen mutinied and sent to Essex, whereupon he came thither.
whereever: At whatever place. He cannot but love virtue, wherever it is.
wherewith: With which. The love wherewith thou hast loved me]ohn 17:26.
With what. Wherewith shall I save Israel? Judges 6:15.
wherret: To hurry; to trouble; to tease; to give a box on the ear.
wherry: A boat used on rivers. The name is given to several kinds of light boats. It is also applied to some decked vessels used in fishing, in different parts of Great Britain and Ireland.
A liquor made from the pulp of crabs after the verjuice is expressed; sometimes called crab-wherry.
whet: pret. and pp. whetted or whet.
To rub for the purpose of sharpening, as an edge tool; to sharpen by attrition; as, to whet a sythe or an ax.
To provoke; to excite; to stimulate; as, to whet the appetite.
To provoke; to make angry or acrimonious. Since Cassius first did whet me against Cesar, I have not slept. To whet on or whet forward, to urge on; to instigate.
The act of sharpening by friction.
whether: Which of two. Whether of them twain did the will of his father? Matthew 21:31.I . Here whether is a substitute for one of two, and signifies which; which of the two; but in this sense it is obsolete.
whetstone: A stone used for sharpening edged instruments by friction.
whetstone-slate: Novaculite or coticular shist, a variety of slate used for sharpening instruments of iron. The light green colored variety from the Levant is the most valuable. It should be kept in a damp place, that it may not become too dry and hard.
whet-slate: Novaculite or coticular shist, a variety of slate used for sharpening instruments of iron. The light green colored variety from the Levant is the most valuable. It should be kept in a damp place, that it may not become too dry and hard.
whetted: Rubbed for sharpening; sharpened; provoked; stimulated.
whetter: He or that which whets or sharpens.
whetting: Rubbing for the purpose of making sharp; sharpening; provoking; inciting; stimulating.
whey: The serum or watery part of milk, separated from the more thick or coagulable part, particularly in the process of making cheese. In this process, the thick part is called curd, and the thin part whey.
whey-tub: A tub in which whey stands for yielding cream
which: A word called a relative or pronoun relative, because it relates to another word or thing, usually to some word that precedes it in the sentence. I call it also a substitute, as it supplies the place of a noun, or of an adjective, or of a sentence or clause. 1. The garden which I cultivate, that is, the garden, which garden I cultivate. 2. We are bound to obey all the divine commands, which we cannot do without divine aid. Here which represents the words, obey the divine commands. 3. You declared him to be innocent, which he is not. Here which stands for innocent. In the foregoing uses, which is not used int eh masculine gender, that is, it does not in modern usage represent a person.

Which is much used in asking questions, for the purpose of obtaining the designation of a particular person or thing by the answer, and in this use, it is of the masculine as well as of the neuter gender. There are two or three things to be done; which shall I do first? Which man is it? Which of you convinceth me of sin? John 8:46. For which of those works do ye stone me? John 10:32.
That which. Take which you will, that is, take any one of the whole. The which, by the which. The use of the before which, is obsolete.
whichever: Whether one or the other. Whichever road you take, it will conduct you to town.
whichsoever: Whether one or the other. Whichever road you take, it will conduct you to town.
whiff: A sudden expulsion of air from the mouth; a puff; as the whiff of a smoker. And seasons his whiffs with impertinent jokes.
In ichthyology, a species of Pleuronectes or flounder.
TO puff; to throw out in whiffs; to consume in whiffs.
whiffle: To start, shift and turn; to change from one opinion or course to another; to use evasions; to prevaricate; to be fickle and unsteady. A person of a whiffing and unsteady turn of mind, cannot keep close to a point of controversy.
To disperse with a puff; to scatter.
Anciently, a fife or small flute.
whifele: To disperse with a puff; to scatter.
whiffele: Anciently, a fife or small flute.
whiffler: One who whiffles or frequently changes his opinion or course; one who uses sifts and evasions in argument.
A harbinger; perhaps one who blows the horn or trumpet.
A young man who goes before a company in London on occasions of public solemnity.
whig: Acidulated whey, sometimes mixed with butter milk and sweet herbs; used as a cooling beverage.

One of a political party which had its origin in England in the seventeenth century, in the reign of Charles I. or Il., when great contests existed respecting the royal prerogatives and the rights of the people. Those who supported the king in his high claims, were called tories, and the advocates of popular rights were called whigs. During the revolution in the United States, the friends and supporters of the war and the principles of the revolution, were called whigs, and those who opposed them, were called tories and royalists. Where then, when tories scarce get clear, shall whigs and congresses appear?
whiggish: Pertaining to whigs; partaking of the principles of whigs.
while: Time; space of time, or continued duration. He was some while in this country. One while we thought him innocent. Pausing a while, thus to herself she musd. Worth while, worth the time which it requires; worth the time and pains; hence, worth the expense. It is not always worth while for a man to prosecute for small debts.
During the time that while I write, you sleep.
As long as. Use your memory, and you will sensibly experience a gradual improvement, while you take care not to overload it.

To while away, as time, in English, is to loiter; or more generally, to cause time to pass away pleasantly, without irksomeness; as, we while away time in amusements or diversions. Let us while away this life.
whiling: Loitering; passing time agreeably, without impatience or tediousness.
whilst: The same as while, which see. Whiles is not used.
whim: Properly, a sudden turn or start of the mind; a freak; a fancy; a capricious notion. We say, every man has his whims. [See Freak and Caprice.,;. All the superfluous whims relate.
A low wit; a cant word.
whimper: To cry with a low, whining, broken voice; as, a child whimpers.
whimpering: Crying with a low broken voice.
whimpled: a word used by Shakespeare, is perhaps a mistake for whimpered. There is no such word in the English.
whimsey: s as . A whim; a freak; a capricious notion; as the whimseys of poets.
s as z. A whim; a freak; a capricious notion; as the whimseys of poets. Mens follies, whimsies, and inconstancy.
whimsical: Full of whims; freakish; having odd fancies; capricious. My neighbors call me whimsical. WIHIMSICAL.
whin: Gorse; furze; a plant of the genus Ulex.
whin-ax: An instrument used for extirpating whin from land.
whin-chat: A bird, a species of warbler, the Motacilla rubetra, Linn.
whine: TO express murmurs by a plaintive cry; to moan with a puerile noise; to murmur meanly. They came--with a whining accent craving liberty: . Then, if we whine, look pale-- .
A plaintive tone; the nasal puerile tone of mean complaint; mean or affected complaint.
whining: Expressing murmurs by a mean plaintive tone or cant.
whinny: To utter the sound of a horse; to neigh.
whinoc: The small pig of a litter.
whin-stone: Whin-stone or whin is a provincial name given to basaltic rocks, and applied by miners to any kind of dark colored and hard unstratified rock which resists the point of the pick. Veins of dark basalt or green-stone, are frequently called whin-dykes.
whip: To strike with a lash or sweeping cord; as, to whip a horse.
To drive with lashes; as, to whip a top.
To punish with the whip; as, to whip a vagrant; to whip one thirty nine lashes; to whip a perverse boy. Who, for false quantities, was whippd at school.
To lash with sarcasm. They would whip me with their fine wits.
whip-cord: Cord of which lashes are made.
whip-graft: To graft by cutting the cion and stock in a sloping direction, so as to fit each other, and by inserting a tongue on the cion into a slit in the stock.
whip-grafting: The at or practice of grafting by cutting the cion and stock with a slope, to fit each other
whip-hand: Advantage over; as, he has the whip-hand of her.
whipped: Struck with a whip; punished; enwrapped; sewed slightly.
whipper: One who whips; particularly, an officer who inflicts the penalty of legal whipping.
whipping: Striking with a whip; punishing with a whip; enwrapping.
The act of striking with a whip, or of punishing; the state of being whipped.
whipping-post: A post to which offenders are tied when whipped.
whipple-tree: The bar to which the traces or tugs of a harness are fastened, and by which a carriage, a plow, a harrow or other implement is drawn.
whippowil: The popular name of an American bird, so called from its note, or the sounds of its voice.
whip-saw: A saw to be used by two persons.
whip-staff: In ships, a bar by which the rudder is turned. In small vessels this is called the tiller.
whip-stitch: In agriculture, to half-plow or to rafter land. This word, I believe, is not used in America. The practice of whip-stitching resembles what is called in America ridging.
whip-stock: The rod or staff to which the lash of a whip is fastened.
whipt: of whip; sometimes used for whipped.
whir: hwur. To whirl round with noise; to fly with noise.
whirl: hwurl. TO turn round rapidly; to turn with velocity. He whirls his sword around without delay.
To be turned round rapidly; to move round with velocity; as the whirling spindles of a cotton machine or wheels of a coach. The wooden engine flies and whirls about.
To move hastily. --But whirld away, to shun his hateful sight.
A turning with rapidity or velocity; rapid rotation or circumvolution; quick gyration; as the whirl of a top; the whirl of a wheel; the whirl of time; the whirls of fancy.
Any thing that moves or is turned with velocity, particularly on an axis or pivot.
whirl-bat: Any thing moved with a whirl as preparatory for a blow, or to augment the force of it. Poets use it for the ancient cestus. The whirl-bat and the rapid race shall be reservd for Cesar.
whirl-bone: The patella; the cap of the knee; the knee-pan.
whirled: In botany, growing in whirls; bearing whirls; verticillate.
whirligig: A toy which children spin or whirl round.

In military antiquities, an instrument for punishing petty offenders, as sutlers, brawling women; a kind of wooden cage turning on a pivot, in which the offender was whirled round with great velocity: .
whirling: Turning or moving round with velocity.
whirling-table: A machine contrived to exhibit and demonstrate the principal laws of gravitation, and of the planetary motion in curvilinear orbits.
whirlpool: An eddy of water; a vortex or gulf where the water moves round in a circle. In some cases, a whirlpool draws things to its center and absorbs them, as is the case with the Maelstrom off the coast of Norway.
whirlwind: A violent wind moving in a circle, or rather in a spiral form, as if moving round an axis; this axis or the perpendicular column moving horizontally, raising and whirling dust, leaves and the like.
whirring: The sound of a partridges or pheasants wings.
whisk: A small bunch of grass, straw, hair or the like, used for a brush; hence, a brush or small besom.
Part of a womans dress; a kind of tippet.
To sweep, brush or wipe with a whisk.
To sweep along; to move nimbly over the ground.
To move nimbly and with velocity.
whisker: Long hair growing on the human cheek.
whiskered: Formed into whiskers; furnished with whiskers.
whisking: Brushing; sweeping along; moving with velocity along the surface.
whisky: A spirit distilled from grain. In the north of England, the name is given to the spirit drawn from barley. In the United States, whisky is generally distilled from wheat, rye or maiz.
whisper: To speak with a low hissing or sibilant voice. It is ill manners to whisper in company. The hollow whispring breeze-- .
To speak with suspicion or timorous caution.
To plot secretly; to devise in mischief. All that hate me whisper together against me. Psalm 41:7.
To address in a low voice. He whispers the man in the ear.
To utter in a low sibilant voice. He whispered a word in my ear.
whispered: Uttered in a low voice; uttered with suspicion or caution.
whisperer: A tattler; one who tells secrets; a conveyer of intelligence secretly.
A backbiter; one who slanders secretly. Proverbs 16:28.
whispering: Speaking in a low voice; telling secretly; backbiting.
The act of speaking with a low voice; the telling of tales, and exciting of suspicions; a backbiting.
whist: Silent; mute; still; not speaking; not making a noise. The winds with wonder whist, smoothly the waters kissd. Whist is used for be silent. Whist, whist, that is, be silent or still .
A game at cards, so called because it requires silence or close attention. It is not in America pronounced whisk.
whistle: To utter a kind of musical sound, by pressing the breath through a small orifice formed by contracting the lips. While the plowman near at hand, whistles oer the furrowd land.
To make a sound with a small wind instrument. \ .
To sound shrill, or like a pipe. The wild winds whistle, and the billows roar.
To form, utter or modulate by whistling; as, to whistle a tune or air.
whistled: Sounded with a pipe; uttered in a whistle.
whistle-fish: A local name of a species of Gadus, with only tow fins on the back; the Mustela fluviatilis.
whistling: Uttering a musical sound through a small orifice of the lips; sounding with a pipe; making a shrill sound, as wind.
whit: A point; a jot; the smallest part or particle imaginable. It is used without a preposition. He is not a whit the wiser for experience. It does not me a whit displease. The regular construction would be by a whit, or in a whit. In these phrases, a whit may be interpreted by in the least, in the smallest degree.
white: Being in the color of pure snow; snowy; not dark; as white paper; a white skin.
Pale; destitute of color in the cheeks, or of the tinge of blood color; as white with fear.
Having the color of purity; pure; clean; free from spot; as white robed innocence.
Gray; as white hair; a venerable man, white with age.
Pure; unblemished. No whiter page than Addisons remains.
white-bait: A very small delicate fish, of the genus Clupea.
white-beam: The white-leaf tree, a species of Crataegus.
white-bear: The bear that inhabits the polar regions.
white-brant: A species of the duck kind, the Anas hyperborea.
white-bug: An insect of the bug kind, which injures vines and some other species of fruit.
white-campion: A pernicious perennial weed, growing in corn land, pastures and hedges.
white-caterpillar: An insect of a small size, called sometimes the borer, that injures the gooseberry bush.
white-centaury: AN annual weed in woods and other places. It is said to form the basis of the famous Portland powder for the gout.
white-clover: A small species of perennial clover, bearing white flowers. It furnishes excellent food for cattle and horses, as well as for the honey bee.
white-crop: White crops, in agriculture, are such as lose their green color or become white in ripening, as wheat, rye, barley and oats.
white-darnel: A prolific and troublesome weed, growing among corm.
white-tail: A bird, the wheat-ear, a species of Motacilla.
white-face: A white mark in the forehead of a horse, descending almost to the nose.
white-blaze: A white mark in the forehead of a horse, descending almost to the nose.
white-film: A white film growing over the eyes of sheep and causing blindness.
white-foot: A white mark on the foot of a horse, between the fetlock and the coffin.
white-honeysuckle: A name sometimes given to the white clover.
white-horse-fish: In ichthyology, the Raia aspera nostras of Willoughby, and the Raia fullonica of Linne. It has a rough spiny back, and on the tail are three rows of strong spines. It grows to the size of the skate.
white-land: A name which the English give to a tough clayey soil, of a whitish hue when dry, but blackish after rain.
white-lead: A carbonate of lead, much used in painting. It is prepared by exposing sheets of lead to the fumes of an acid, usually vinegar, and suspending them in the air until the surface becomes incrusted with a white coat, which is the substance in question.
white-line: Among printers, a void space, broader than usual, left between lines.
white-livered: Having a pale look; feeble; cowardly.
white-manganese: An ore of manganese; carbonated oxydized manganese.
white-meat: Meats made of milk, butter, cheese, eggs and the like.
white-poplar: A tree of the poplar kind, sometimes called the abele tree.
white-poppy: A species of poppy, sometimes cultivated for the opium which is obtained from its juice by evaporation.
white-pot: A kind of food made of milk, cream, eggs, sugarbaked in a pot.
white-pyrite: An ore of a tin-white color, passing into a brass-yellow and steel-gray, occurring in octahedral crystals, sometimes stalactitical and botryoidal. It contains 46 parts of iron, and 54 of sulphur.
white-pyrites: An ore of a tin-white color, passing into a brass-yellow and steel-gray, occurring in octahedral crystals, sometimes stalactitical and botryoidal. It contains 46 parts of iron, and 54 of sulphur.
white-rent: In Devon and Cornwall, a rent or duty of eight pence, payable yearly by every tinner to the duke of Cornwall, as lord of the soil.
white-salt: Salt dried and calcined; decrepitated salt.
whitestone: IN geology, the weiss stein of Werner, and the eurite of some geologists; a species of rocks, composed essentially of feldspar, but containing mica and other minerals.
white-swelling: A swelling or chronic enlargement of the joints, circumscribed, without any alteration in the color of the skin, sometimes hard, sometimes yielding to pressure, sometimes indolent, but usually painful.
white-thorn: A species of thorn, called also haw-thorn, of the genus Crataegus.
white-throat: A small bird that frequents gardens and hedges, the Motacila sylvia.
white-vitriol: In mineralogy, sulphate of zink, a natural salt.
whitewash: Awash or liquid composition for whitening something; a wash for making the skin fair.
A composition of lime and water, used for whitening the plaster of walls.
To cover with a white liquid composition, as with lime and water.
To make white; to give a fair external appearance.
whitewashed: Covered or overspread with a white liquid composition.
white-washer: One who whitewashes the walls or plastering of apartments.
whitewashing: Overspreading or washing with a white liquid composition.
white-water: A disease of sheep, of the dangerous stomachic kind.
white-wine: Any wine of a clear transparent color, bordering on white, as Madeira, Sherry, Lisbon; opposed to wine of a deep red color, as Port and Burgundy.
whitewood: A species of timber tree growing in North America, the Liriodendron, or tulip tree. The name of certain species of Bignonia.
whiten: hwitn. To make white; to bleach; to blanch; as, to whiten cloth.
To grow white; to turn or become white. The hair whitens with age; the sea whitens with foam; the trees in spring whiten with blossoms.
whitener: One who bleaches or makes white.
whiteness: The state of being white; white color, or freedom from any darkness or obscurity on the surface.
Paleness; want of a sanguineous tinge in the face.
Purity; cleanness; freedom from stain or blemish.
whites: The fluor albus, a disease of females.
whither: To what placeWhither goest thou? Whither away so fast?
To what place. I strayd, I knew not whither.
To which place. Whither when as they came, they fell at words.
whithersoever: To whatever place. I will go whithersoever you lead.
whiting: A small sea fish, the Asellus mollis or albus, a species of Gadus.
The same as Spanish white, which see.
whitish: Somewhat white; white in a moderate degree.
whitishness: The quality of being somewhat white.
whitleather: Lether dressed with alum, remarkable for its toughness. In common use, the ligaments of animals, when in food.
whitlether: Lether dressed with alum, remarkable for its toughness. In common use, the ligaments of animals, when in food.
whitlow: In surgery, paronychia, a swelling or inflammation about the nails or ends of the fingers, or affecting one or more of the phalanges of the fingers, generally terminating in an abscess. There are four or five varieties of this swelling. 1. The cutaneous paronychia, which raises the cuticle, forming a kind of vesicle filled with a limpid serum, or bloody fluid. 2. The subcutaneous paronychia, a tumor attended with acute pain. It is seated in the cellular membrane under the skin. 3. The subungual paronychia, which occurs under the nail. It commences with inflammatory symptoms, but is less painful than the former. 4. There is also the paronychia of the periosteum, and the paronychia of the tendons or theca.
In sheep, the whitlow is a disease of the feet, of an inflammatory kind. It occurs round the hoof, where an acrid matter is collected, which ought to be discharged.
whitlow-grass: Mountain knotgrass, a species of Illecebrium.
A name given to certain species of Draba. The rue-leaved whitlow-grass is a species of Saxifraga.
whitsul: A provincial name of milk, sour milk, cheese curds and butter.
whitsuntide: The feast or season of Pentecost; so called it is said, because, in the primitive church, those who had been newly baptized appeared at church between Easter and Pentecost in white garments.
whittle: A white dress for a woman; a double blanket worn by west countrywomen in England, over the shoulders, like a cloke.
To pare or cut off the surface of a thing with a small knife. Some persons have a habit of whittling, and are rarely seen without a penknife in their hands for that purpose.
whity-brown: Of a color between white and brown.
whiz: To make a humming or hissing sound, like an arrow or ball flying through the air. It flew, and whizzing cut the liquid way.
whizzing: Making a humming or hissing sound.
who: Who is a pronoun relative, always referring to persons. It forms whose in the genitive or possessive case, answering to the L. Cujus, and whom in the objective or accusative case. Who, whose and whom, are in both numbers. Thus we say, the man or woman who was with us; the men or women who were with us; the men or women whom we saw.
Which of many. Are you satisfied who did the mischief?
It is much used in asking questions; as, who am I? Who art thou? Who is this? Who are these? In this case, the purpose is to obtain the name or designation of the person or character.
Ithas sometimes a disjunctive sense. There thou tellst of kings, and who aspire; who fall, who rise, who triumph, who do moan.
whoever: Any on without exception; any person whatever. The person who trespasses shall be punished, whoever he may be.
whole: All; total; containing the total amount or number, or the entire thing; as the whole earth; the whole world; the whole solar system; the whole army; the whole nation.
Complete; entire; not defective or imperfect; as a whole orange; the egg is whole; the vessel is whole.
Unimpaired; unbroken; uninjured. My life is yet whole in me. 2 Samuel 1:9.
Sound; not hurt or sick. They that are whole need not a physician. Matthew 9:12.
wholesale: Sale of goods by the piece or large quantity; as distinguished from retail. Some traders sell either by wholesale or retail.
The whole mass. Some from vanity or envy, despise a valuable book, and throw contempt upon it by wholesale.
Buying and selling by the piece or quantity; as a wholesale merchant or dealer.
Pertaining to the trade by the piece or quantity; as the wholesale price.
wholesome: Tending to promote health; favoring health; salubrious; as wholesome air or diet; a wholesome — climate.
Sound; contributing to the health of the mind; favorable to morals, religion or prosperity; as wholesome advice; wholesome doctrines; wholesome truths.
Useful; salutary; conducive to public happiness, virtue or peace; as a wholesome law.
That utters sound words. A wholesome tongue is a tree of life. Proverbs 15:4.
Kindly; pleasing; as a wholesome answer. Wholesome ship, a ship that will try, hull and ride well.
wholesomely: In a wholesome or salutary manner; salubriously.
wholesomeness: The quality of contributing to health; salubrith; as the wholesomeness of air or diet.
Salutariness; conduciveness to the health of the mind or of the body politic; as the wholesomeness of doctrines or laws.
wholly: Entirely; completely; perfectly. Nor wholly overcome, nor wholly yield.
Totally; in all the parts or kinds. They employed themselves wholly in domestic life.
whom: The objective of who, coinciding with the L. quem and quam. Whom have I in heaven but thee? Psalm 73:25.
whomsoever: Any person without exception. With whomsoever thou findest thy goods, let him not live. Genesis 31:32.
whoop: A shout of war; a particular cry of troops when they rush to the attack. The Indians of America are remarkable for their war whoop.
The bird called hoopoe or upupa.
To shout with a particular voice.
whop: A sudden fall, or the suddenness of striking in a fall.
whore: Hore. A harlot; a courtesan; a concubine; a prostitute.
To have unlawful sexual commerce; to practice lewdness.
whoreson: A bastard; a word used generally in contempt.
whorish: Lewd; unchaste; addicted to unlawful sexual pleasures; incontinent.
whorishness: The practice of lewdness; the character of a lewd woman.
whort: The fruit of the whortleberry; or the shrub.
whortleberry: A plant or shrub and its fruit, of the genus Vaccinium.
whose: The possessive or genitive case of who or which; applied to persons or things. We say, the person whose merits are known; the garment whose color is admired.
whosoever: Any one; any person whatever. Whosoever will, let him take of the water of life freely. Revelation 22:17.
whur: To pronounce the letter r with too much force.
The sound of a body moving through the air with velocity.
why: For what cause or reason. Turn ye, turn ye, for why will ye die? Jeremiah 27:13.
For which reason or cause. No ground of enmity, why he should mean me ill.
For what reason or cause; for which; relatively. Turn the discourse; I have a reason why I would not have you speak so tenderly.
It is used sometimes emphatically, or rather as a expletive. If her chill heart I cannot move, why, Ill enjoy the very love.
wi: from the Gothic weiha, signifies holy. It is found in some names, as in Wibert, holy- bright, or bright-holy, eminent for sanctity.
wic: a termination, denotes jurisdiction, as in bailiwick. Its primary sense is a village or mansion, L.; hence it occurs in Berwick, Harwich, NorwichIt signifies also a bay or a castle.
wick: a termination, denotes jurisdiction, as in bailiwick. Its primary sense is a village or mansion, L.; hence it occurs in Berwick, Harwich, NorwichIt signifies also a bay or a castle.

A number of threads of cotton or some similar substance, loosely twisted into a string, round which wax or tallow is applied by means of melting and running in a mold, and thus forming a candle or torch.
wicked: Evil in principle or practice; deviating from the divine law; addicted to vice; sinful; immoral. This is a word of comprehensive signification, extending to every thing that is contrary to the moral law, and both to persons and actions. We say, a wicked man, a wicked deed, wicked ways, wicked lives, a wicked heart, wicked designs, wicked works. No man was ever wicked without secret discontent.
Aword of slight blame; as the wicked urchin.

Cursed; baneful; pernicious; as wicked words, words pernicious in their efforts. [This last signification may throw some light on the word witch. The wicked, in Scripture, persons who live in sin; transgressors of the divine law; all who are unreconciled to God, unsanctified or impenitent.
wickedly: IN a manner or with motives and designs contrary to the divine law; viciously; corruptly; immorally. All that do wickedly shall be stubble. Valachi 4:7. I have sinned, and I have done wickedly. 2 Samuel 24:17.
wickedness: Departure from the rules of the divine law; evil disposition or practices; immorality; crime; sin; sinfulness; corrupt manners Wickedness generally signifies evil practices. What wickedness is this that is done among you? Judges 20:12. But wickedness expresses also the corrupt dispositions of the heart. Their inward part is very wickedness. Psalm 5:9. In heart ye work wickedness. Psalm 58:2.
wicken: The Sorbus aucuparia, mountain ash, or roan-tree.
wicken-tree: The Sorbus aucuparia, mountain ash, or roan-tree.
wicker: Made of twigs or oziers; as a wicker basket; a wicker chair.
wicket: A small gate. The wicket, often opend, knew the key.
wickliffite: A follower of Wickliffe, the English reformer.
wide: Broad; having a great or considerable distance or extent between the sides; opposed to narrow; as wide cloth; a wide table; a wide highway; a wide bed; a wide hall or entry. In this use, wide is distinguished from long, which refers to the extent or distance between the ends.
Broad; having a great extent each way; as a wide plain; the wide ocean.
Remote; distant. This position is very wide from the truth.
Broad to a certain degree; as three feet wide.
At a distance; far. His fame was spread wide.
widely: With great extent each way. The gospel was widely disseminated by the apostles.
Very much; to a great distance; far. We differ widely in opinion.
widen: To make wide or wider; to extend in breadth; as, to widen a field; to widen a breach.
To grow wide or wider; to enlarge; to extend itself. And arches widen, and long aisles extend.
widened: Made wide or wider; extended in breadth.
wideness: Breadth; width; great extent between the sides; as the wideness of a room.
Large extent in all directions; as the wideness of the sea or ocean.
widening: Extending the distance between the sides; enlarging in all directions.
widgeon: A fowl of the duck kind, or genus Anas, having a black bill, the head and upper part of the neck of a bright bay, the back and sides waved with black and white, and the belly white.
widow: A woman who has lost her husband by death. Luke 2:37. Widows chamber, in London, the apparel and furniture of the bed- chamber of the widow of a freeman, to which she is entitled.
To bereave of a husband; but rarely used except in the participle.
To endow with a widows right.
To strip of any thing good. The widowd isle in mourning-- .
widow-bench: In Sussex, that share which a widow is allowed of her husbands estate, besides her jointure.
widowed: Bereaved of a husband by death.
Deprived of some good; stripped. Trees of their shriveld fruits are widowd.
widower: A man who has lost his wife by death.
widowhood: The state of being a widow.
widow-hunter: One who seeks or courts widows for a jointure or fortune.
widowing: Bereaving of a husband; depriving; stripping.
widow-maker: One who make widows by destroying lives.
widow-wail: In botany, a plant of the genus Cneorum.
width: Breadth; wideness; the extent of a thing from side to side; as the width of cloth; the width of a door.
wield: To use with full command or power, as a thing not too heavy for the holder; to manage; as, to wield a sword; to wield the scepter. Part wield their arms, part curb the foaming steed.
To use or employ with the hand. Nothing but the influence of a civilized power could induce a savage to wield a spade.
To handle; in an ironical sense. Base Hungarian wight, wilt thou the spigot wield? To wield the scepter, to govern with supreme command.
wiery: Made of wire; having the properties of wire. It would be better written wiry.
wife: The lawful consort of man; a woman who is united to man in the lawful bonds of wedlock; the correlative of husband. WIIFE. The husband of one wife. 7 Timothy 3:2, 12. Let every one of you in particular, so love his wife even as himself, and let the wife see that she reverence her husband. Ephesians 5:33.
A woman of low employment; as strawberry wives.
wig: in Saxon, signifies war. It is found in some names.
A covering for the head, consisting of hair interwoven or united by a kind of network; formerly much worn by men.
wight: A being; a person. It is obsolete, except in irony or burlesque. The wight of all the world who lovd thee best.
wigwam: An Indian cabin or hut, so called in America. It is sometimes written weekwam.
wild: Roving; wandering; inhabiting the forest or open field; hence, not tamed or domesticated; as a wild boar; a wild ox; a wild cat; a wild bee.
Growing without culture; as wild parsnep; wild cherry; wild tansy. Wild rice, a palatable and nutritious food, grows spontaneously in the lakes and ponds of the North West territory.
Desert; not inhabited; as a wild forest.
Savage; uncivilized; not refined by culture; as the wild natives of Africa or America.
Turbulent; tempestuous; irregular; as a wild tumult. The wild winds howl.
wildfire: Acomposition of inflammable materials. Brimstone, pitch, wildfire, burn easily, and are hard to quench.
A disease of sheep, attended with inflammation of the skin; a kind of erysipelas.
wild-fowl: Fowls of the forest, or untamed.
wild-goose: An aquatic fowl of the genus Anas, the Anas anser, a fowl of passage. These geese fly to the south in autumn, and return to the north in the spring. This species is the stock of the common domestic goose. The wild goose of North America, also migratory, is a distinct species, the Anas canadensis. Wild-goose chase, the pursuit of something as unlikely to be caught as the wild goose.
wild-honey: Honey that is found int he forest, in hollow trees or among rocks.
wild-land: Land not cultivated, or in a state that renders it unfit for cultivation.
In America, forest; land not settled and cultivated.
wild-service: A plant. The wilder myrtle-leaved service is a tree of the genus Crataegus,
wilder: To lose or cause to lose the way or track; to puzzle with mazes or difficulties; to bewilder. Long lost and wilderd in the maze of fate.
wildered: Lost in a pathless tract; puzzled.
wilderness: A desert; a tract of land or region uncultivated and uninhabited by human beings, whether a forest or a wide barren plain. In the United States, it is applied only to a forest. In Scripture, it is applied frequently to the deserts of Arabia. The Israelites wandered int he wilderness forty years.
The ocean. The watry wilderness yields no supply. WIILDERNESS.
Awood in a garden, resembling a forest.t
wildly: With disorder; with perturbation or distraction; with a fierce or roving look; as, to start wildly from ones seat; to stare wildly.
Capriciously; irrationally; extravagantly. Who is there so wildly skeptical as to question whether the sun will rise in the east?
wildness: Rudeness; rough uncultivated state; as the wildness of a forest or heath.
Inordinate disposition to rove; irregularity of manners; as the wildness of youth. WIILDNESS.
Uncultivated state; as the wildness of land.
wilds: Among farmers, the part of a plow by which it is drawn.
wile: A trick or stratagem practiced for ensnaring or deception; a sly, insidious artifice. That ye may be able to stand against the wiles of the devil. Ephesians 6:11.
wilily: By stratagem; with insidious art. Joshua 9:4.
will: [G., L., Gr. The sense is to set, or to set forward, to stretch forward. The sense is well expressed by the

That faculty of the mind by which we determine either to do or forbear an action; the faculty which is exercised in deciding, among two or more objects, which we shall embrace or pursue. The will is directed or influenced by the judgment. The understanding or reason compares different objects, which operate as motives; the judgment determines which is preferable, and the will decides which to pursue. In other words, we reason with respect to the value or importance of things; we then judge which is to be preferred; and we will to take the most valuable. These are but different operations of the mind, soul, or intellectual part of man. Great disputes have existed respecting the freedom of the will. Will is often quite a different thing from desire. A power over a man’s subsistence, amounts to a power over his will.
Choice; determination. It is my will to prosecute the trespasser.
Choice; discretion; pleasure. Go, then, the guilty at thy will chastise.
Command; direction. Our prayers should be according to the will of God.
willed: Disposed of by will or testament.
willful: Governed by the will without yielding to reason; obstinate; stubborn; perverse; inflexible; as a willful man.
Stubborn; refractory; as a willful horse.
willfully: By design; with set purpose. If we sin willfully after that we have received the knowledge of the truth, there remaineth no more sacrifice for sins. Hebrews 10:26.
willfulness: Obstinacy; stubbornness; perverseness. Sins of presumption are such as proceed from pride, arrogance, willfulness, and haughtiness of mens heart.
willing: Free to do or grant; having the mind inclined; disposed; not averse. Let every man give, who is able and willing.
Pleased; desirous. Felix, willing to show the Jews a pleasure. Acis 24:27.
Ready; prompt. He stoopd with weary wings and willing feet.
willing-hearted: Well disposed; having a free heart. Exodus 35:5.
willingly: With free will; without reluctance; cheerfully.
By ones own choice. The condition of that people is not so much to be envied as some would willingly represent it.
willingness: Free choice or consent of the will; freedom from reluctance; readiness of the mind to do or forbear. Sweet is the love that comes with willingness.
willow: A tree of the genus Salix. There are several species of willow, the white, the black, the purple or red, the sallow, and the broad leaved willowA species called the weeping willow, has long and slender branches which droop and hang downward, the Salix Babylonica.
willow-gall: A protuberance on the leaves of willows.
willow-herb: The purple loose strife, a plant of the genus Lythrum; also, the yellow loose strife, of the genus Lysimachia; also, the French willow, of the genus Epilobium.
willowish: Like the color of the willow.
willow-weed: A name sometimes given to the smartweed or persicaria.
wilt: To begin to wither; to lose freshness and become flaccid, as a plant when exposed to great heat in a dry day, or when first separated from its root. This is a legitimate word, for which there is no substitute in the language. It is not synonymous with wither, as it expresses only the beginning of withering. A wilted plant often revives and becomes fresh; not so a withered plant.
To cause to begin to wither; to make flaccid; as a green plant.
To cause to languish; to depress or destroy the vigor and energy of. Despots have wilted the human race into’ sloth and imbecility.
wilted: Having become flaccid and lost its freshness, as a plant.
wily: Cunning; sly; using craft or stratagem to accomplish a purpose; subtle; as a wily adversary.
wimble: An instrument for boring holes, turned by a handle.
wimbrel: A bird of the curlew kind, a species of Soclopax.
wimple: To draw down, as a vail.
win: To gain by success in competition or contest; as, to win the prize in a game; to win money; to win a battle, or to win a country. Battles are won by superior strength or skill. --Who thus shall Canaan win.
To gain by solicitation or courtship.
To obtain; to allure to kindness or compliance. Thy virtue won me. Win your enemy by kindness.
To gain by persuasion or influence; as, an orator wins his audience by argument. The advocate has won the jury. And Mammon wins his way, where seraphs might despair.
wince: To shrink, as from a blow or from pain; to start back. I will not stir nor wince.
To kick or flounce when uneasy, or impatient of a rider; as, a horse winces.
wincer: One that winces, shrinks or kicks.
winch: A windlass; or an instrument with which to turn or strain something forcibly; as a winch to strain the cord of a bedstead, or to turn a wheel.
To wince; to shrink; to kick with impatience or uneasiness.
wincopipe: The vulgar name of a little flower, that, when it opens in the morning, bodes a fair day.
wind: Air in motion with any degree of velocity, indefinitely; a current of air. When the air moves moderately, we call it a light wind, or a breeze; when with more velocity, we call it a fresh breeze, and when with violence, we call it a gale, storm or tempest. The word gale is used by the poets for a moderate breeze, but seamen use it as equivalent to storm. Winds are denominated from the point of compass from which they blow; as a north wind; an east wind; a south wind; a west wind; a southwest wind.

The four winds, the cardinal points of the heavens. Come from the four winds, O breath, and breathe upon these slain. Ezekiel 37:9. This sense of the word seems to have had its origin with the orientals, as it was the practice of the Hebrews to give to each of the four cardinal points the name of wind.
Direction of the wind from other points of the compass than the cardinal, or any point of compass; as a compass of eight winds.
Breath; power of respiration. If my wind were but long enough to say my prayers, I would repent.
Air in motion form any force or action; as the wind of a cannon ball; the wind of a bellows.
windage: The difference between the diameter of a piece and that of a ball or shell.
windbound: Prevented from sailing by a contrary wind.
wind-dropsy: A swelling of the belly from wind in the intestines; tympauites.
winder: To fan; to clean grain with a fan.
winder-meb: A bird of the genus Larus, or gull-kind.
windfall: Fruit blown off the tree by wind.
wind-furnace: A furnace in which the air is supplied by an artificial current, as from a bellows.
wind-gage: A soft tumor on the fetlock joints of a horse.
wind-gun: An air gun; a gun discharged by the force of compressed air.
wind-hatch: In mining, the opening or place where the ore is taken out of the earth.
wind-hover: A species of hawk; called also the stannel, but more usually the kestrel.
windiness: The state of being windy or tempestuous; as the windiness of the weather or season.
Tendency to generate wind; as the windiness of vegetables.
Tumor; puffiness. The swelling windiness of much knowledge.
wind-instrument: An instrument of music, played by wind, chiefly by the breath; as a flute, a clarinet
windlas: A machine for raising great weights, consisting of a cylinder or roller of timber, moving on its axis and turned by levers, with a rope or chain attached to the weight.
A handle by which any thing is turned.
windlass: A machine for raising great weights, consisting of a cylinder or roller of timber, moving on its axis and turned by levers, with a rope or chain attached to the weight.
A handle by which any thing is turned.
windle: A spindle; a kind of reel.
wind-mill: A mill turned by the wind.
windpipe: The passage for the breath to and from the lungs; the trachea.
wind-pump: A pump moved by wind, useful in draining lands.
wind-rode: A term used by seamen to signify a ship when riding with wind and tide opposed to each other, driven to the leeward of her anchor.
windrow: A row or line of hay, raked together for the purpose of being rolled into cocks or heaps.
The green border of a field, dug up in order to carry the earth on other land to mend it.
A row of peats set up for drying; or a row of pieces of turf, sod or sward, cut in paring and burning.
wind-sail: A wide tube or funnel of canvas, used to convey a stream of air into the lower apartments of a ship.
windseed: A plant of the genus Arctolis.
wind-shock: A sort of bruise or shiver in a tree.
wind-tight: So tight as to prevent the passing of wind.
windward: The point from which the wind blows; as, to ply to the windward.
Being on the side towards the point from which the wind blows; as the windward shrouds.
windy: Consisting of wind; as a windy tempest.
Next the wind; as the windy side.
winding: a. Bending; twisting from a direct line or an even surface.
A turn or turning; a bend; flexure; meander; as the windings of a road or stream.
A call by the boastswains whistle.
winding-engine: An engine employed in mining, to draw up buckets from a deep pit.
winding-sheet: A sheet in which a corpse is wrapped.
winding-tackle: A tackle consisting of one fixed triple block, and one double or triple movable block.
window: An opening in the wall of a building for the admission of light, and of air when necessary. This opening has a frame on the sides, in which are set movable sashes, containing panes of glass. In the United Sates, the sashes are made to rise and fall, for the admission or exclusion of air. In France, windows are shut with frames or sashes that open and shut vertically, like the leaves of a folding door.
An aperture or opening. A window shalt thou make to the ark. Genesis 6:76.
The frame or other thing that covers the aperture. Ere I let fall the windows of mine eyes.
An aperture; or rather the clouds or water-spouts. The windows of heaven were opened. Genesis 7:11.
Lattice or casement; or the network of wire used before the invention of glass. Judges 5:28.
window-blind: A blind to intercept the light of a window. Venetian window-blinds are now much used in the United States.
window-frame: The frame of a window which receives and holds the sashes.
window-sash: The sash or light frame in which panes of glass are set for windows.
windowy: Having little crossings like the sashes of a window.
wine: The fermented juice of grapes; as the wine of the Madeira grape; the wine of Burgundy or Oporto.
The juice of certain fruits, prepared with sugar, spirits; as currant wine; gooseberry wine.
Intoxication. Noah awoke from his wine. Genesis 9:24.

Drinking. They that tarry long at the wine. Proverbs 23:30. Corn and wine, in Scripture, are put for all kinds of necessaries for subsistence. Psalm. Bread and wine, in the Lords supper, are symbols of the body and blood of Christ.
wine-bibber: One who drinks much wine; a great drinker. Proverbs 23:20.
wine-cask: A cask in which wine is or has been kept.
wine-fly: A small fly found in empty wine casks.
wine-glass: A small glass in which wine is drank.
wineless: Destitute of wine; as wineless life.
wine-measure: The measure by which wines and other spirits are sold, smaller than beer measure.
wine-merchant: A merchant who deals in wines.
wine-press: A place where grapes are pressed.
wing: The limb of a fowl by which it flies. In a few species of fowls, the wings do not enable them to fly; as is the case with the dodo, ostrich, great auk, and penguin; but in the two former, the wings assist the fowls in running.
The limb of an insect by which it flies.

In botany, the side petal of a papilionaceous corol; also, an appendage of seeds, by means of which they are wafted in the air and scattered; also, any membranous or leafy dilatation of a footstalk, or of the angles of a stem, branch or flower stalk, or of a calyx.
Flight; passage by the wind; as, to be on the wind; to take wing.
Means of flying; acceleration. Fear adds wings to flight .
winged: Furnished with wings; transported by flying. 2.a. Having wings; as a winged fowl. Genesis 1:21.
Swift; rapid; as with winged haste.

In botany, furnished with longitudinal membranous appendages, as a winged stalk or stem; or with downy or hairy appendages, as winged seeds. Winged petiole, having a thing membrane or border on each side, or dilated on the sides. Winged leaf, a pennate leaf; a species of compound leaf, wherein a simple leaf has several leaflets fastened to each side of it.
In heraldry, represented with wings, or having wings of a different color from the body.
wingless: Having no wings; not able to ascend or fly.
wing-shell: The shell that covers the wing of insects.
wingy: Having wings; rapid; as wingy speed.
wink: To shut the eyes; to close the eyelids. They are not blind, but they wink.
To close and open the eyelids.
To give a hint by a motion of the eyelids. Wink at the footman to leave him without a plate.
To close the eyelids and exclude the light. Or wink as cowards and afraid.
To be dim; as a winking light. To wink at, to connive at; to seem not to see; to tolerate; to overlook, as something not perfectly agreeable; as, to wink at faults.
winking: Shutting the eyes; shutting and opening the eyelids; hinting by closing the eye; conniving at; overlooking.
winner: One who gains by success in competition or contest.
winning: Gaining success in competition or contest. 2.a. Attracting; adapted to gain favor; charming; as a winning address.
The sum won or gained by success in competition or contest.
winnow: To separate and drive off the chaff from grain by means of wind. Grain is winnowed by a fan, or by a machine, or by pouring it out of a vessel in a current of air.
To fan; to beat as with wings.
To examine; to sift for the purpose of separating falsehood from truth. Winnow well this thought.
To separate, as the bad from the good.
To separate chaff from corn. Winnow not with every wind.
winnowed: Separated from the chaff by wind; sifted; examined.
winnowing: Separating from the chaff by wind; examining.
winter: The cold season of the year. Astronomically considered, winter commences in northern latitudes when the sun enters Capricorn, or at the solstice about the 21st of December, and ends at the equinox in March; but in ordinary discourse, the three winter months are December, January, and February. Our Saxon ancestors reckoned the years by winters; as ten winters; thirty winters. In tropical climates, there are two winters annually; but they cannot be said to be cold. In the temperate and frigid climates, there is one winter only in the year.
The part of the printing press which sustains the carriage.
To pass the winter. He wintered in Italy. Cattle winter well on good fodder.
To feed or manage during the winter. To winter young cattle on straw, is not profitable. Delicate plants must be wintered under cover.
winter-apple: An apple that keeps well in winter.
winter-barley: A kind of barley which is sowed in autumn.
winter-beaten: Harassed by the severe weather of winter.
winter-berry: A plant of the genus Prinos.
winter-bloom: A plant of the genus Azalea.
winter-cherry: A plant of the genus Physalis, and its fruit, which is of the size of a cherry.
winter-cress: A plant of the genus Erysimum.
winter-crop: A crop which will bear the winter, or which may be converted into fodder during the winter.
winter-fallow: Ground that is fallowed in winter.
winter-green: A plant of the genus Pyrola, useful as a vulnerary.
winter-kill: To kill by means of the weather in winter; as, to winter-kill wheat or clover.
To be killed by the winter. Wheat is liable to winter-kill in moist land.
winter-killed: Killed by the winter, as grain.
winter-killing: Killing by the weather in winter.
winter-lodge: In botany, the hybernacle of a plant, which protects the embryo or future shoot from injuries during the winter. It is either a bud or a bulb.
winter-lodgment: In botany, the hybernacle of a plant, which protects the embryo or future shoot from injuries during the winter. It is either a bud or a bulb.
winter-pear: Any pear that keeps well in winter.
winter-quarters: The quarters of an army during the winter; a winter residence or station.
winter rig: To fallow or till in winter.
winter-solstice: The solstice of the winter, which takes place when the sun enters Capricorn, December 21st.
wintering: Passing the winter; keeping in winter.
winterly: Such as is suitable to winter.
wintery: Suitable to winter; brumal; hyemal; cold; stormy.
winy: Having the taste or qualities of wine.
wipe: To rub something soft for cleaning; to clean by rubbing; as, to wipe the hands or face with a towel. Luke 7:38.
To strike off gently. Some natral tears they droppd, but wipd them soon.
To cleanse from evil practices or abuses; to overturn and destroy what is foul and hateful. I will wipe Jerusalem as a man wipeth a dish. 2 Kings 27:3.

To cheat; to defraud. To wipe away, to cleanse by rubbing or tersion; as, to wipe away a stain or reproach. To wipe off, to clear away. Wipe off this foul stain; wipe off the dust. To wipe out, to efface; to obliterate. Wipe out the blot.
The act of rubbing for the purpose of cleaning.
wiped: Rubbed for cleaning; cleaned by rubbing; cleared away; effaced.
wiping: Rubbing with a cloth or other soft thing for cleaning; clearing away; effacing.
wire: A thread of metal; any metallic substance drawn to an even thread.
To bind with wire; to apply wire to, as in bottling liquors.
wiredraw: To draw a metal into wire, which is done by drawing it through a hole in a plate of steel.
To draw by art or violence. My sense has’ been’ wiredrawn into blasphemy.
To draw or spin out to great length and tenuity; as, to wiredraw an argument.
wiredrawer: One who draw metal into wire.
wiredrawing: Drawing to a great length or fineness.
wiredrawn: Drawn into wire; drawn out to great length or fineness.
wire-grate: A grate or contrivance of fine wire work to keep insects out of vineries, hot houses
wire-heel: A defect and disease in the feet of a horse or other beast.
wire-worm: A mischievous worm that sometimes injures grain.
wis: pret. wist. To think; to suppose; to imagine.
wisdom: The right use or exercise of knowledge; the choice of laudable ends, and of the best means to accomplish them. This is wisdom in act, effect, or practice. If wisdom is to be considered as a faculty of the mind, it is the faculty of discerning or judging what is most just, proper and useful, and if it is to be considered as an acquirement, it is the knowledge and use of what is best, most just, most proper, most conducive to prosperity or happiness. Wisdom in the first sense, or practical wisdom, is nearly synonymous with discretion. I differs somewhat from prudence, in this respect; prudence is the exercise of sound judgment in avoiding evils; wisdom is the exercise of sound judgment either in avoiding evils or attempting good. Prudence then is a species, of which wisdom is the genus. Wisdom gained by experience, is of inestimable value. It is hoped that our rulers will act with dignity
In Scripture, human learning; erudition; knowledge of arts and sciences. Moses was learned in all the wisdom of the Egyptians. Acts 7:22.
Quickness of intellect; readiness of apprehension; dexterity in execution; as the wisdom of Bezaleel and Aholiab. Exodus 31:2-
wise: Properly, having knowledge; hence, having the power of discerning and judging correctly, or of discriminating between what is true and what is false; between what is fit and proper, and what is improper; as a wise prince; a wise magistrate. Solomon was deemed the wisest man. But a man may be speculatively and not practically wise. Hence,
Discrete and judicious in the use or applications of knowledge; choosing laudable ends, and the best means to accomplish them. This is to be practically wise. Genesis 41:33.
Skillful; dextrous. /ISE. They are wise to do evil, but to do good they have no knowledge. Jeremiah 4:22.
Learned; knowing; as the wise and the unwise. Romans 1:14.
Skilled in arts, science, philosophy, or in magic and divination. Samuel 14:2.
wiseacre: More correctly wisesager. One who makes pretensions to great wisdom; hence in contempt, a simpleton; a dunce.
wiseling: One who pretends to be wise.
wisely: Prudently; judiciously; discretely; with wisdom. Proverbs 16:20; Proverbs 21:12.
Craftily; with art or stratagem. Let us deal wisely with them. Exodus 1:10.
wish: To have a desire, or strong desire, either for what is or is not supposed to be obtainable. It usually expresses less than long; but sometimes it denotes to long or wise earnestly. We often wise for what is not obtainable. This is as good an argument as an antiquary could wish for. They have more than heart could wish. Psalm 73:7. I wish above all things that thou mayest prosper. 3 John 2. They cast four anchors out of the stern, and wished for the day. Acts 27:29.
To be disposed or inclined; as, to wish well to anothers affairs.
It sometimes partakes of hope or fear. I wish the event may prove fortunate, or less calamitous than we apprehend.
To desire. I wish your prosperity. Let them be driven backward and put to shame, that wish me evil. Psalm 40:14.
To long for; to desire eagerly or ardently. It has this sense when expressed with emphasis.
wisher: One who desires; one who expresses a wish.
wisp: A small bundle of straw or other like substance; as a wisp of straw; a wisp of hay; a wisp of herbs.
wistful: Full of thoughts; earnest; attentive. Why--dost thou so wistful seem?
wistit: The striated monkey; a small species of monkey from South America, with an annulated tail, the ouistiti of Buffon.
wit: To know. This verb is used only in the infinitive, to wit, namely, that is to say.
Primarily, the intellect; the understanding or mental powers. N . Will puts in practice what the wit deviseth. For wit and power their last endeavors bend t outshine each other.

The association of ideas in a manner natural, but unusual and striking, so as to produce surprise joined with pleasure. Wit is defined. What oft was thought, but neer so well expressd. Wit consists in assembling and putting together with quickness, ideas in which can be found resemblance and congruity, by which to make up pleasant pictures and agreeable visions in the fancy. Wit consists chiefly in joining things by distant and fanciful relations, which surprise us because they are unexpected. Wit is a propriety of thoughts and words; or in other terms, thoughts and words elegantly adapted to the subject.
The faculty of associating ideas in a new and unexpected manner.
A man of genius; as, the age of Addison abounded with wits. A wit herself, Amelia weds a wit.
witch: A woman who by compact with the devil, practices sorcery or enchantment.
A woman who is given to unlawful arts.
To bewitch; to fascinate; to enchant. Ill witch sweet ladies with my words and looks.
witchcraft: The practices of witches; sorcery; enchantments; intercourse with the devil.
Power more than natural. He hath a witchcraft over the king ins tongue.
witch-hazel: The hop-hornbeam, (carpinus ostrya.) . The Virginian witch-hazel is the Hamemelis virginica, a shrub which flowers in autumn when its leaves are falling.
wit-cracker: One who breaks jests; a joker.
wit-fish: An East Indian fish of the size of a whiting; also, another East Indian fish, the Albula Indica of Ray.
with: By, noting cause, instrument or means. We are distressed with pain; we are elevated with joy. With study men become learned and respectable. Fire is extinguished with water.
On the side of, noting friendship or favor. Fear not, for I am with thee. Genesis 26:24.
In opposition to; in competition or contest; as, to struggle with adversity. The champions fought with each other an hour. He will lie with any man living.
Noting comparison. The fact you mention compares well with another I have witnessed.
In company. The gentlemen traveled with me from Boston to Philadelphia.
withe: A band consisting of a twig, or twigs twisted.
