withal: With the rest; together with; likewise; at the same time. If you choose that, then I am yours withal. How modest in exception, and withal how terrible in constant resolution!
It is sometimes used for with. But the word is not elegant, nor much used.
withdraw: To take back; to take from. It is impossible that God should withdraw his presence from any thing. We say, to withdraw capital from a bank or stock in trade, to withdraw aid or assistance.
To recall; to cause to retire or leave; to call back or away. France has withdrawn her troops from Spain.
To retire; to retreat; to quit a company or place. We withdrew from the company at ten o’clock. She from her husband soft withdrew.
withdrawing-room: A room behind another room for retirement; a drawing room.
withdrawment: The act of withdrawing or taking back; a recalling. Their withdrawment from the British and Foreign Bible Society, would tend to paralyze their exertions.
wither: To fade; to lose its native freshness; to become sapless; to dry. It shall wither in all the leaves of her spring. Ezekiel 17:9.
To waste; to pine away; as animal bodies; as a withered hand. Matthew 12:10.
To lose or want animal moisture. Now warm in love, now withring in the grave.
To cause to fade and become dry; as, the sun withereth the grass. James 1:11.
To cause to shrink, wrinkle and decay, for want of animal moisture. Age cannot wither her.
wither-band: A piece of iron laid under a saddle near a horses withers, to strengthen the bow.
witherite: In mineralogy, a carbonate of baryte, first discovered by Dr. Withering; rhomboidal baryte. It is white, gray, or yellow.
withernam: In withernam, in law, a second or reciprocal distress, in lieu of a first distress which has been eloigned; reprisal.
withers: The juncture of the shoulder bones of a horse, at the bottom of the neck.
wither-wrung: Injured or hurt in the withers, as a horse.
withhold: TO hold back; to restrain; to keep from action. Withhold--your hasty hand. If our passions may be withheld.
To retain; to keep back; not to grant; as, to withhold assent to a proposition. The sun does not withhold his light.
withholden: The old participle of withhold; now obsolete. We use withheld.
withholding: Holding back; restraining; retaining; not granting.
within: In the inner part; as the space within the walls of a house; a man contented and happy within himself.
In the limits or compass of; not beyond; used of place and time. The object is within my sight; within the knowledge of the present generation; within a month or a year.
Not reaching to any thing external. Were every action concluded within itself-- .
In the compass of; not longer ago than. Within these five hours Hastings livd untainted.
Not later than; as, within five days from this time, it will be fair weather.
without: In a state of destitution or absence from. There is no living with thee nor without thee.
Beyond; not within. Eternity, before the world and after, is without our reach.
Supposing the negation or omission of. Without the separation of the two monarchies, the most advantageous terms from the French must end in our destruction.
Independent of; not by the use of. Men like to live without labor. Wise men will do it without a law.
withouten: for withoutan, the Saxon word, is obsolete.
withstand: To oppose; to resist, either with physical or moral force; as, to withstand the attack of troops; to withstand eloquence or arguments. When Peter was come to Antioch, I withstood him to his face. Galatians 2:11.
withstander: One that opposes; an opponent; a resisting power.
with-vine: A local name for the couch-grass.
with-wine: A local name for the couch-grass.
witless: Destitute of wit or understanding; inconsiderate; wanting thought; as a witless swain; witless youth.
Indiscrete; not under the guidance of judgment; as witless bravery.
witling: A person who has little wit or understanding; a pretender to wit or smartness. A beau and witling perishd in the throng.
witness: Testimony; attestation of a fact or event. If I bear witness of myself, my witness is not true. John 5:37.
That which furnishes evidence or proof. Laban said, this heap is a witness between me and thee this day. Genesis 31:48.
A person who knows or sees any thing; one personally present; as, he was witness; he was an eye-witness. 7 Peter 5:7.
One who sees the execution of an instrument, and subscribes it for the purpose of confirming its authenticity b his testimony.

One who gives testimony; as, the witnesses in court agreed in all essential facts. With a witness, effectually; to a great degree; with great force, so as to leave some mark as a testimony behind. He struck with a witness.
witnessed: Seen in person; testified; subscribed by persons present; as a deed witnessed by two persons.
witnessing: Seeing in persons; bearing testimony; giving evidence.
wit-starved: Barren of wit; destitute of genius.
witted: Having wit or understanding; as a quick witted boy.
witticism: A sentence or phrase which is affectedly witty; a low kind of wit. --He is full of conceptions, points of epigram, and witticisms; all which are below the dignity of heroic verse.
wittily: With wit; with a delicate turn or phrase, or with an ingenious association of ideas.
Ingeniously; cunningly; artfully. Who his own harm so wittily contrives.
wittingly: Knowingly; with knowledge; by design. He knowingly and wittingly brought evil into the world.
wittol: A man who knows his wifes infidelity and submits to it; a tame cuckold.
witty: Possessed of wit; full of wit; as a witty poet.
Sarcastic; full of taunts. WITT Y. Honeycomb was unmercifully witty upon the women.
witwall: A bird, the great spotted woodpecker.
wizard: A conjurer; an enchanter; a sorcerer. Leviticus 20:27. The wily wizard must be caught.
wo: Grief; sorrow; misery; a heavy calamity. One woe is past; and behold, there come two woes more hereafter. Revelation 9:12. They weep each others wo.
A curse. Can there be a wo or curse in all the stores of vengeance, equal to the malignity of such a practice?

Wo is used in denunciation, and in exclamations of sorrow. Wo is me; for I am undone. Isaiah 6:5. This is properly the Saxon dative, wo is to me. Wo worth the day. This is also the dative; wo be to the day. Wo is a noun, and if used as an adjective, it is improperly used. Wo to you that are rich. Wo to that man, by whom the offense cometh; that is, misery, calamity, be or will be to him.
woe: Grief; sorrow; misery; a heavy calamity. One woe is past; and behold, there come two woes more hereafter. Revelation 9:12. They weep each others wo.
A curse. Can there be a wo or curse in all the stores of vengeance, equal to the malignity of such a practice?

Wo is used in denunciation, and in exclamations of sorrow. Wo is me; for I am undone. Isaiah 6:5. This is properly the Saxon dative, wo is to me. Wo worth the day. This is also the dative; wo be to the day. Wo is a noun, and if used as an adjective, it is improperly used. Wo to you that are rich. Wo to that man, by whom the offense cometh; that is, misery, calamity, be or will be to him.
woad: A plant of the genus Isatis, cultivated for the use of dyers. The woad blue is a very deep blue, and is the base of many other colors or shades of color. Woad is first bruised in a mill, and then made into balls. It grows wild in France and along the coasts of the Baltic. The term woad is applied to the Reseda, weld or wold, and to the Genista tinctoria or dyers broom.
woad-mill: A mill for bruising and preparing woad.
wobegone: Overwhelmed with wo; immersed in grief and sorrow. So wobegone was he with pains of love.
wodanium: A metal recently discovered in a species of pyrite, found in Hungary, which had been supposed to be an ore of cobalt. It has a bronze yellow color. Vi
woful: Sorrowful; distressed with grief or calamity; afflicted. How many woful widows left to bow to sad disgrace!
Sorrowful; mournful; full of distress; as woful day. Jeremiah 17:16.
Bringing calamity, distress or affliction; as a woful event; woful want.
Wretched; paltry. What woful stuff this madrigal would be.
wofully: Sorrowfully; mournfully; in a distressing manner.
Wretchedly; extremely; as, he will be wofully deceived.
wolf: An animal of the genus Canis, a beast of prey that kills sheep and other small domestic animals; called sometimes the wild dog. The wolf is crafty, greedy and ravenous.
A small white worm or maggot, which infests granaries.
wolf-dog: A dog of a large breed, kept to guard sheep.
A dog supposed to be bred between a dog and a wolf.
wolf-fish: A fish, the lupus marinus, (the Anarrhichas lupus of Linne;) a fierce voracious fish of the northern seas.
wolfish: Like a wolf; having the qualities or form of a wolf; as a wolfish visage; wolfish designs.
wolf-net: A kind of net used in fishing, which takes great numbers.
wolfram: In mineralogy, an ore of tungsten. Its color is generally a brownish of grayish black; when cut with a knife, it gives a reddish brown streak. It occurs massive and crystalized, and in concentric lamellar concretions.
wolverin: The glutton, a carnivorous animal of voracious appetite. The name wolverene is applied to an animal of North America, considered by Linne as a peculiar species, (Ursus luscus,) cut which has been since regarded as a variety of the glutton, (U. Gulo.)
wolverene: The glutton, a carnivorous animal of voracious appetite. The name wolverene is applied to an animal of North America, considered by Linne as a peculiar species, (Ursus luscus,) cut which has been since regarded as a variety of the glutton, (U. Gulo.)
woman: The female of the human race, grown to adult years. And the rib, which the Lord God had taken from the man, made he a woman. Genesis 2:22. Women are soft, mild, pitiful, and flexible. We see every day women perish with infamy, by having been too willing to set their beauty to show. I have observed among all nations that the women ornament themselves more than the men; that wherever found, they are the same kind, civil, obliging, humane, tender beings, inclined to be gay and cheerful, timorous and modest.
womaned: Accompanied or united with a woman.
woman-hater: One who has an aversion to the female sex.
womanhood: the state, character or collective qualities of a woman.
womanish: Suitable to a woman; having the qualities of a woman; feminine; as womanish habits; womanish tears; a womanish voice.
womankind: The female sex; the race of females of the human kind.
womanly: Becoming a woman; feminine; as womanly behavior. A blushing womanly discovering grace.
In the manner of a woman.
womb: The uterus or matrix of a female; that part where the young of an animal is conceived and nourished till its birth.
The place where any thing is produced. The womb of earth the genial seed receives.
Any large or deep cavity. Womb of the morning, in Scripture, the clouds, which distill dew; supposed to be emblematic of the church bringing forth multitudes to Christ. Psalm 110:3.
To inclose; to breed in secret.
wombat: An animal of New Holland, of the opossum family.
women: of woman. pron. wimen. But it is supposed the word we pronounce is from Sax. wifman, and therefore should be written wimen.
won: and pp. of win; as victories won.
To dwell; to abide. Obsolete. Its participle is retained in wont, that is
wone: To dwell; to abide. Obsolete. Its participle is retained in wont, that is
wonder: That emotion which is excited by novelty, or the presentation to the sight or mind, of something new, unusual, strange, great, extraordinary, or not well understood; something that arrests the attention by its novelty, grandeur or inexplicableness. Wonder expresses less than astonishment, and much less than amazement. It differs from admiration, in not being necessarily accompanied with love, esteem or approbation, nor directed to persons. But wonder sometimes is nearly allied to astonishment, and the exact extent of the meaning of such words can hardly be graduated. They were filled with wonder and amazement. Acts 3:10. Wonder is the effect of novelty upon ignorance. W.
Cause of wonder; that which excites surprise; a strange thing; a prodigy. To try things oft, and never to give over, doth wonders. I am as a wonder to many. Psalm 77:7.

Any thing mentioned with surprise. Babylon, the wonder of all tongues. Wonders of the world. The seven wonders of the world were the Egyptian pyramids, the Mausoleum erected by Artemisia, the temple of Diana at Ephesus, the walls and hanging gardens of Babylon, the colossus at Rhodes, the statue of Jupiter Olympius, and the Pharos or watch-tower of Alexandria. IONDER.
To be affected by surprise or admiration. I could not sufficiently wonder at the intrepidity of these diminutive mortals. We cease to wonder at what we understand. IONDER.
wonderful: Adapted to excite wonder or admiration; exciting surprise; strange; astonishing. Job 42:3.
wonderfully: In a manner to excite wonder or surprise. I will praise thee, for I am fearfully and wonderfully made. Psalm 139:14.
wonderfulness: The state or quality of being wonderful.
wondering: Indulging or feeling wonder. Genesis 24:21; Luke 24:12.
wonderstruck: Struck with wonder, admiration and surprise.
wondrous: Admirable; marvelous; such as may excite surprise and astonishment; strange. That I may publish with the voice of thanksgiving, and tell of all thy wondrous works. Psalm 26:7.

In a wonderful or surprising degree; as a place wondrous deep; you are wondrous fair; wondrous fond of peace. These phrases of Cowley, Dryden and Pope, are admissible only in the ludicrous and burlesque style.
wondrously: In a strange or wonderful manner or degree. Chloe complains, and wondrouslys aggrievd.
wont: a contraction of woll not, that is, will not.
Accustomed; habituated; using or doing customarily. If the ox were wont to push with his horn-- Exodus 21:29. They were wont to speak in old time, saying-- 2 Samuel 20:18; Matthew 27:15; Luke 22:39.
To be accustomed or habituated; to be used. A yearly solemn feast she wont to make. Wherewith he wont to soar s high.
wonted: Accustomed; used. Again his wonted weapon provd.
Accustomed; made familiar by use. She was wonted to the place, and would not remove.
wontless: To court; to solicit in love. WOO. My proud rival wooes another partner to his throne and bed--\/
woo: Each, like the Grecian artist, wooes the image he himself has wrought.
To court solicitously; to invite with importunity. Thee, chantress, oft the woods among, I woo to hear thy even song.
wood: A large and thick collection of trees; a forest. Light thickens, and the crow makes wing to the rooky wood.
The substance of trees; the hard substance which composes the body of a tree and its branches, and which is covered by the bark.
Trees cut or sawed for the fire. Wood is yet the principal fuel in the United States.
wood-ashes: The remains of burnt wood or plants.
wood-bind: A name given to the honeysuckle, a species of Lonicera.
wood-bine: A name given to the honeysuckle, a species of Lonicera.
woodchuk: The popular name in New England of a species of the Marmot tribe of animals, the Arctomys monax. It burrows and is dormant in winter.
wood-cock: A fowl of the genus Scolopax, inhabiting the northern parts of the European continent in summer, but frequenting England in winter. The wood cock of the United States is a smaller species.
wood-cockshell: A name given by English naturalists to a peculiar kind of the purpura, called by the French becasse; of two species, the prickly and the smooth.
wood-drink: A decoction or infusion of medicinal woods.
wooded: Supplied or covered with wood; as land wooded and watered.
wooden: Made of wood; consisting of wood; as a wooden box; a wooden leg; a wooden horse.
Clumsy; awkward. When a bold man is put out of countenance, he makes a very wooden figure on it. W.
wood-engraving: Xylography; the art of engraving on wood, or of cutting figures of natural objects on wood.
wood-fretter: An insect or worm that eats wood.
wood-hole: A place where wood is laid up.
woo-house: A house or shed in which wood is deposited and sheltered from the weather.
wood-land: Land covered with wood, or land on which trees are suffered to grow, either for fuel or timber.
In England, a soil which, from its humidity and color, resembles the soil in woods.
wood-lark: A bird, a species of lark.
wood-layer: A young oak or other timber plant, laid down in a hedge among the white thorn or other plants used in hedges.
wood-lock: In shipbuilding, a piece of elm, close fitted and sheathed with copper, in the throating or score of the pintle, to keep the rudder from rising.
woodman: A forest officer, appointed to take care of the kings wood.
wood-meil: A coarse hairy stuff made of Iceland wool, used to line the ports of ships of war.
wood-mite: A small insect found in old wood.
wood-mote: In England, the ancient name of the forest court; now the court of attachment.
wood-note: Wild music. --Or sweetest Shakespeare, fancys child, warble his native wood- notes wild.
wood-nymph: A fabled goddess of the woods; adryad.
A fabled goddess of the woods; a dryad. The wood-nymphs deckd with daisies trim.
wood-offering: Wood burnt on the altar. Nehemiah 10:34.
woodpecker: A bird of the genus Picus, that pecks holes in trees, or that picks insects form the bark.
wood-puceron: A small insect of the puceron kind, of a grayish color, having two hollow horns on the hinder part of its body. It resembles the puceron of the alder, but it penetrates into the wood.
woodreve: In England, the steward or overseer of awood.
wood-roof: A plant of the genus Asperula.
wood-ruff: A plant of the genus Asperula.
wood-sage: A plant of the genus Teucrium.
wood-sare: A kind of froth seen on herbs.
wood-seere: The time when there is no sap in a tree.
wood-shock: The fisher or wejack, a quadruped of the weasel kind in North America.
wood-soot: Soot from burnt wood, which has been found useful as a manure.
wood-sorrel: A plant of the genus Oxalis.
wood-spite: A name given in some parts of England to the green woodpecker.
wood-stone: A blackish gray silicious stone, a subspecies of horn-stone.
wood-ward: An officer of the forest, whose duty is to guard the woods.
wood-wash: A name sometimes applied to dyers broom.
woodwaxen: A plant of the genus Genista; dyers broom.
wood-worm: A worm that is bread in wood.
woody: Abounding with wood; as woody land; a woody region. --Secret shades of woody Idas inmost grove. WIOODY.
Consisting of wood; ligneous; as the woody parts of plants.
Pertaining to woods; sylvan; as woody nymphs.
wooer: One who courts, or solicits in love.
woof: The threads that cross the warp in weaving; the weft.
Texture; cloth; as a pall of softest woof.
wooingly: Enticingly; with persuasiveness; so as to invite to stay.
wool: That species of hair which grows on sheep and some other animals, which in fineness sometimes approaches to fur. The word generally signifies the fleecy coat of the sheep, which constitutes a most essential material of clothing in all cold and temperate climates.
In botany, a sort of pubescence, or a clothing o dense curling hairs on the surface of certain plants.
wool-ball: A ball or mass of wool found in the stomach of sheep.
wool-comber: One whose occupation is to comb wool.
woold: To wind, particularly to wind a rope round a mast or yard, when made of two or more pieces, at the place where they are fished, for confining and supporting them.
woolded: Bound fast with ropes; wound round.
woolding: Binding fast with ropes; winding round.
The act of winding, as a rope round a mast. WIOOLDING.
The rope used for binding masts and = spars.
wool-driver: One who buys wool and carries it to market.
woolen: Made of wool; consisting of wool; as woolen cloth.
Pertaining to wool; as woolen manufactures. IOOLEN.
woolen-draper: One who deals in woolen goods.
woolfel: A skin with the wool; a skin from which the wool has not been sheared or pulled.
woolly: Consisting of wool; as a woolly covering; a woolly fleece.
Clothed with wool; as woolly breeders.
In botany, clothed with a pubescence resembling wool.
woolly-pastinum: A name given in the East Indies to a species of red orpiment or arsenic.
woolpack: A pack or bag of wool. W.
woolsack: A sack or bag of wool.
The seat of the lord chancellor and of the judges in the house of lords.
wool-staple: A city or town where wool used to be brought to the kings staple for sale.
wool-winder: A person employed to wind or make up wool into bundles to be packed for sale.
woots: Indian steel, a metallic substance imported form the East Indies; valued as the material of edge-tools. It has in combination a minute portion of alumin and silica.
word: An articulate or vocal sound, or a combination of articulate and vocal sounds, uttered by the human voice, and by custom expressing an idea or ideas; a single component part of human speech or language. Thus a in English is a word; but few words consist of one letter only. Most words consist of tow or more letters, as go, do, shall, called monosyllables, or of two or more syllables, as honor, goodness.
The letter or letters, written or printed, which represent a sound or combination of sounds.
A short discourse. Shall I vouchsafe your worship a word or two?
Talk; discourse. Why should calamity be full of words? Be thy words severe.
Dispute; verbal contention; as, some words grew between us.
wordiness: The state or quality of abounding with words.
wording: The act of expressing in words.
The manner of expressing in words. The wording of the ideas is very judicious.
wordless: Not using words; not speaking; silent.
wordy: Using many words; verbose; as a wordy speaker; a wordy orator.
Containing many words; full of words. We need not lavish hours in wordy periods. WIORDY.
work: In a general sense, to move, or to move one way and the other; to perform; as in popular language it is said, a mill or machine works well.
To labor; to be occupied in performing manual labor, whether severe or moderate. One man works better than another; one man works hare; another works lazily.
To be in action or motion; as the working of the heart.
To act; to carry on operations. Our better part remains to work in close design.
To operate; to carry on business; to be customarily engaged or employed in. Some work in the mines, others in the loom, others at the anvil. They that work in fine flax. Isaiah 19:9.
worker: One that works; one that performs.
work-fellow: One engaged in the same work with another. Romans 16:21.
workhouse: A house where any manufacture is carried on.
Generally, a house in which idle and vicious persons are confined to labor.
working-house: A house where any manufacture is carried on.
Generally, a house in which idle and vicious persons are confined to labor.
working: Movement; operation; as the workings of fancy.
workman: Any man employed in labor, whether in tillage or manufactures.
By way of eminence, a skillful artificer or laborer.
workmanly: In a skillful manner; in a manner becoming a workman.
workmanship: Manufacture; something made, particularly by manual labor. Exodus 31:5.
That which is effected, made or produced. Ephesians 2:10.
The skill of a workman; or the execution or manner of making any thing. The workmanship of this cloth is admirable.
workshop: A shop where any manufacture is carried on.
workwoman: A woman who performs any work; or one skilled in needle work.
world: The universe; the whole system of created globes or vast bodies of matter.
The earth; the terraqueous globe; sometimes called the lower world.
The heavens; as when we speak of the heavenly world, or upper world.

System of beings; or the orbs which occupy space, and all the beings which inhabit them. Hebrews 11:3. God--hath in these last days spoken to us by his Son, whom he hath appointed heir of all things; by whom also he made the worlds. Hebrews 1:2. There may be other worlds, where the inhabitants have never violated their allegiance to their Almighty sovereign.
Present state of existence; as while we are in the world. Behold, these are the ungodly who prosper in the world. Psalm 73:12.
worldliness: A predominant passion for obtaining the good things of this life; covetousness; addictedness to gain and temporal enjoyments.
worldling: A person whose soul is set upon gaining temporal possessions; one devoted to this world and its enjoyments. If we consider the expectations of futurity, the worldling gives up the argument.
worldly: Secular; temporal; pertaining to this world or life, in contradistinction to the life to come; as worldly pleasures; worldly affairs; worldly estate; worldly honor; worldly lusts. Titus 2:12.
Devoted to this life and its enjoyments; bent on gain; as a worldly man; a worldly mind.
Human; common; belonging to the world; as worldly actions; worldly maxims.
With relation to this life. Subverting worldly strong and worldly wise by simply meek.
worldly-minded: Devoted to the acquisition of property and to temporal enjoyments.
worldly-mindedness: A predominating love and pursuit of this worlds goods, to the exclusion of piety and attention to spiritual concerns.
worm: In common usage, any small creeping animal, or reptile, either entirely without feet, or with very short ones, including a great variety of animals of different classes and ordersCertain small serpents, as the blind-worm or slow-worm; the larvas of insectsGrubs, caterpillars and maggots, as the wood-worm, canker-worm, silk-worm, (the larva of a moth (Phaloena,) which spins the filaments of which silk is made,) the grub that injures corn, grass, the worms that breed in putrid flesh, the bots in the stomach of horses, and many others; certain wingless insects, as the glow- worm; the intestinal worms, or such as breed in the cavities and organs of living animals, as the tape-worm, the round-worm, the fluke; and numerous animals found in the earth, and in water, particularly in the sea, as the earth-worm or lumbricus, the hair- worm or gordius, the teredo, or worm that bores in to the bottom

In zoology, the term Vermes or worms has been applied to different divisions of invertebral animals, by different naturalists. Linnes class of vermes, includes the following ordersIntestina, including the proper intestinal worms the earth-worm, the hair- worm, the teredo, and some other marine worms; Mollusca, including the slug, and numerous soft animals inhabiting the water, particularly the sea; Testacea, including all the proper shell-fish; Zoophyta or compound animals, including corals, polypes, and spunges; and Infusoria, or simple microscopic animlacules. His character of the class is, --spiracles obscure, jaws various, organs of sense usually tentacula, no brain, ears nor nostrils, limbs wanting, frequently hermaphrodite. This class includes all the invertebral animals, except the insects and crustacea. The term Vermes has been since greatly limited, particularly by the French na
Remorse; that which incessantly gnaws the conscience; that which torments. Where their worm dieth not. Mark 9:44.
A being debased and despised. lam aworm, and no man. Psalm 22:6.
A spiral instrument or iron screw, used for drawing wads and cartridges from cannon or small arms.
worm-eaten: Gnawed by worms; as worm-eaten boards, planks or timber.
wormed: Cleared by a worm or screw.
worm-grass: A plant of the genus Spigalia.
worming: Entering by insinuation; drawing, as a cartridge; clearing, as a gun.
worm-powder: A powder used for expelling worms from the stomach and intestines.
worm-seed: A seed which has the property of expelling worms from the stomach, bowels, and intestines. It is said to be brought from Persia, and to be the produce of a species of Artemisia.
A plant of the genus Chenopodium. WIORM-SEED.
worm-tincture: A tincture prepared from earth-worms dried, pulverized and mixed with oil of tartar, spirit of wine, saffron and castor.
wormwood: A plant, the artemisia. It has a bitter nauseous taste; but it is stomachic and corroborant. Tree-wormwood, a species of Artemisia, with woody stalks.
wormwood-fly: A small black fly, found on the stalks of wormwood.
worn: of wear; as a garment long worn. Worn out, consumed or rendered useless by wearing.
wornil: A maggot that infests the backs of cows.
worral: An animal of the lizard kind, about four feet long and eight inches broad, with a forked tongue. It feeds on flies, and is harmless. It is found in Egypt.
worry: To tease; to trouble; to harass with importunity, or with care and anxiety. Persons are often worried with care and solicitude. Let them rail and then worry one another at their pleasure. Worry him out till he gives his consent. A church worried with reformation.
To fatigue; to harass with labor; a popular sense of the word.N .
To harass by pursuit and barking; as, dogs worry sheep.
To tear; to mangle with the teeth.
worse: More evil; more bad or ill; more depraved and corrupt; in a moral sense. Evil men and seducers shall wax worse and worse. 2 Timothy 3:13. There are men who seem to believe they are not bad, while another can be found worse.
In a physical sense, in regard to health, more sick. She was nothing bettered, but rather grew worse. Mark 5:26.
More bad; less perfect or good. This carriage is worse for wear. The worse,
The loss; the disadvantage. Judah was put to the worse before Israel. 2 Kings 14:12.
Something less good. Think not the worse of him for his enterprise.
worser: is a vulgar word, and not used in good writing or speaking.
worship: Excellence of character; dignity; worth; worthiness. --Elfin born of noble state, and muckle worship in his native land. In this sense, the word is nearly or quite obsolete; but hence,
A title of honor, used in addresses to certain magistrates and other of respectable character. W. My father desires your worships company.

Chiefly and eminently, the act of paying divine honors to the Supreme Being; or the reverence and homage paid to him in religious exercises, consisting in adoration, confession, prayer, thanksgiving and the like. The worship of God is an eminent part of religion . Prayer is a chief part of religious worship.
The homage paid to idols or false gods by pagans; as the worship or Isis.
worshiped: Adored; treated with divine honors; treated with civil respect.
worshiper: One who worships; one who pays divine honors to any being; one who adores.
worshipful: Claiming respect; worthy of honor from its character or dignity . This is worshipful society.
A term of respect, sometimes ironically. WIMORSHIPFUL.
worshiping: Adoring; paying divine honors to; treating with supreme reverence; treating with extreme submission.
worst: Most bad; most evil; in a moral sense; as the worst man; the worst sinner.
Most severe or dangerous; most difficult to heal; as the worst disease.
Most afflictive, pernicious or calamitous; as the worst evil that can befall a state or an individual.
The most evil state; in a moral sense.
The most severe or aggravated state; the highth; as, the disease is at the worst.
worsted: WUSTED. Yarn spun from combed wool; a particular kind of woolen yarn.
Consisting of worsted; made of worsted yarn; as worsted stockings.
wort: A plant; an herb; now used chiefly or wholly in compounds; as in mugwort, liverwort.
A plant of the cabbage kind.
New beer unfermented, or in the act of fermentation; the sweet infusion of malt.
worth: Termination, signifies a farm or court; as in Wordsworth.
This verb is now used only in the phrases, wo worth the day, wo worth the man, in which the verb is in the imperative mode, and the noun in the dative; wo be to the day.

Value; that quality of a thing which renders it useful, or which will produce an equivalent good in some other thing. The worth of a days labor may be estimated in money, or in wheat. The worth of labor is settled between the hirer and the hired. The worth of commodities is usually the price they will bring in market; but price is not always worth.

Value of mental qualities; excellence; virtue; usefulness; as a man or magistrate of great worth. As none but she, who in that court did dwell, could know such worth, or worth describe so well. All worth-consists in doing good, and in the disposition by which it is done.
Importance; valuable qualities; applied to things; as, these things have since lost their worth.
worthily: In a manner suited to; as, to walk worthily of our extraction.
Deservedly; according to merit. You worthily succeed not only to the honors of your ancestors, but also to their virtues.
Justly; not without cause. I affirm that some may very worthily deserve to be hated.
worthiness: Desert; merit. The prayers which our Savior made, were for his own worthiness accepted.
Excellence; dignity; virtue. Who is sure he hath a soul, unless it see and judge and follow worthiness?
Worth; quality or state of deserving.
worthless: Having no value; as a worthless garment; a worthless ship.
Having no value of character or no virtue; as a worthless man or woman.
Having no dignity or excellence; as a worthless magistrate.
worthlessness: Want of value; want of useful qualities; as the worthlessness of an old garment or of barren land.
Want of excellence or dignity; as the worthlessness of a person.
worthy: Deserving; such as merits; having worth or excellence; equivalent; with of, before the thing deserved. She has married a man worthy of her. Thou art worthy of the sway. I am not worthy of the least of all the mercies-- Genesis 32:10.

Possessing worth or excellence of qualities; virtuous; estimable; as a worthy citizen; a worthy magistrate. Happier thou mayst be, worthier canst not be. This worthy mind should worthy things embrace./\ WORTH Y.
Suitable; having qualities suited to; either in a good or bad sense; equal in value; as flowers worthy of paradise.
Suitable to any thing bad. The merciless Macdonald, worthy to be a rebel.
Deserving of ill; as things worthy of stripes. Luke 12:48.
would: of will, G., L. Would is used as an auxiliary verb in conditional forms of speech. I would go, if I could. This form of expression denotes will or resolution, under a condition or supposition. You would go, He would go, denote simply an event, under a condition or supposition. The condition implied in would is not always expressed. By pleasure and pain, I would be understood to mean what delights or molests us--; that is, if it should be asked what I mean by pleasure and pain, I would thus explain what I wish to have understood. In this form of expression, which is very common, there seems to be an implied allusion to an inquiry, or to the supposition of something not expressed. IOULD. Would has the sense of wish or pray, particularly in the phrases, would to God, would God we had died in Egypt, I would that ye knew what conflict I have; that is, I could wish such a thing, if the wish co
wud: of will, G., L. Would is used as an auxiliary verb in conditional forms of speech. I would go, if I could. This form of expression denotes will or resolution, under a condition or supposition. You would go, He would go, denote simply an event, under a condition or supposition. The condition implied in would is not always expressed. By pleasure and pain, I would be understood to mean what delights or molests us--; that is, if it should be asked what I mean by pleasure and pain, I would thus explain what I wish to have understood. In this form of expression, which is very common, there seems to be an implied allusion to an inquiry, or to the supposition of something not expressed. IOULD. Would has the sense of wish or pray, particularly in the phrases, would to God, would God we had died in Egypt, I would that ye knew what conflict I have; that is, I could wish such a thing, if the wish co
wound: A breach of the skin and flesh of an animal, or of the bark and wood of a tree, or of the bark and substance of other plants, caused by violence or external force. The self-healing power of living beings, animal or vegetable, by which the parts separated in wounds, tend to unite and become sound, is a remarkable proof of divine benevolence and wisdom.
Injury; hurt; as a wound given to credit or reputation.
To hurt by violence; as, to wound the head or the arm; to wound a tree. He was wounded for our transgressions. Isaiah 53:5.
woundwort: The name of several plants; one, a species of Achillea; another, a species of Stachys; another, a species of Laserpitium; another, a species of Solidago; and another a species of Senecio.
wr: Note. W before r is always silent.
wrack: A name given to a marine plant which is of great utility as a manure. It is called sometimes sea-wrack or sea-wreck, and sea-oak and sea- tangle. It is the Fucus vesiculosus of Linne, a plant found on rocks left dry at low water. The stalk runs along the middle of the leaf, and is terminated by watery bladders. The grass wrack is of the genus Zostera. Wrack, and to wrack.
wreck: A name given to a marine plant which is of great utility as a manure. It is called sometimes sea-wrack or sea-wreck, and sea-oak and sea- tangle. It is the Fucus vesiculosus of Linne, a plant found on rocks left dry at low water. The stalk runs along the middle of the leaf, and is terminated by watery bladders. The grass wrack is of the genus Zostera. Wrack, and to wrack.
Destruction; properly, the destruction of a ship or vessel on the shore. Hence,
The ruins of a ship stranded; a ship dashed against rocks or land and broken, or otherwise rendered useless by violence and fracture.
Dissolution by violence; ruin; destruction. The wreck of matter and the crush of worlds.
The remains of any thing ruined; dead weeds and grass.
wrangle: To dispute angrily; to quarrel peevishly and noisily; to brawl; to altercate. For a score of kingdoms you should wrangle. He did not know what it was to wrangle on indifferent points.
An angry dispute; a noisy quarrel.
wrangler: An angry disputant; one who disputes with heat or peevishness; as a noisy contentious wrangler. Senior wrangler, in the university of Cambridge, the student who passes the best examination in the senate house. Then follow the second, thirdWranglers.
wrap: pret. and pp. wrapped or wrapt.
To wind or fold together. John 20:7.
To involve; to cover by winding something round; often with up; as, to wrap up a child in its blanket; wrap the body well with flannel in winter. I, wrapt in mist of midnight vapor, glide obscure.
To involve; to hide; as truth wrapt in tales.
To comprise; to contain. Leontines young wife, in whom all his happiness was wrapped up, died in a few days after the death of her daughter. WIRAP.
wrapper: That in which any thing is wrapped or inclosed.
wrapping: Winding; folding; involving; inclosing. 2.a. Used or designed for wrapping or covering; as wrapping paper.
wrass: A fish, the Labrus tinca of Linne, called by authors, turdus vulgaris, or tinca marina, the sea-tench, and sometimes old-wife. It resembles the carp in figure, and is covered with large scales. The name is also applied to other species of the genus Labrus.
wrasse: A fish, the Labrus tinca of Linne, called by authors, turdus vulgaris, or tinca marina, the sea-tench, and sometimes old-wife. It resembles the carp in figure, and is covered with large scales. The name is also applied to other species of the genus Labrus.
wrath: Violent anger; vehement exasperation; indignation; as the wrath of Achilles. When the wrath of king Ahasuerus was appeased-- Esther 2:1. O Lord--in wrath remember mercy. Habakkuk 3:2,
The just punishment of an offense or crime. Romans 13:4. Gods wrath, in Scripture, is his holy and just indignation against sin. Romans 1:18.
wrathful: Very angry; greatly incensed. The king was very wrathful.
Springing from wrath, or expressing it; as wrathful passions; a wrathful countenance.
wreak: To execute; to inflict; to hurl or drive; as, to wreak vengeance on an enemy. On me let death wreak all his rage. WIREAK.
To revenge. Come wreak his loss, whom bootless ye complain. Anothers wrongs to wreak upon thyself.
for reck. To care, is a mistake.
wreath: To twist; to convolve; to wind one about another; as, to wreath a garland of flowers.
To interweave; to entwine; as chains of wreathed work.
To encircle, as a garland. The flowrs that wreath the sparkling bowl.
To encircle as with a garland; to dress in a garland. And with thy winding ivy wreaths her lance.
To be interwoven or entwined; as a bower of wreathing trees.
wreathy: Twisted; curled; spiral; as a wreathy spire.
wrecked: Dashed against the shore or on rocks; stranded and ruined.
wrecking: Stranding; running on rocks or on shore; ruining.
wren: A small bird of the genus Motacilla.
wrench: To pull with a twist; to wrest, twist or force by violence; as, to wrench a sword from anothers hand.
To strain; to sprain; to distort. You wrenched your foot against a stone.
A violent twist, or a pull with twisting.
A sprain; an injury by twisting; as in a joint.
An instrument for screwing or unscrewing iron work.
wrest: To twist or extort by violence; to pull or force from by violent wringing or twisting; as, to wrest an instrument from anothers hands.
To take or force from by violence. The enemy made a great effort, and wrested the victory from our hands. But fate has wrested the confession from me.

To distort; to turn from truth or twist from its natural meaning by violence; to pervert. Wrest once the law to your authority. Thou shalt not wrest the judgment of the poor. Exodus 23:6. Which they that are unlearned and unstable wrest, as they do also the other Scriptures, to their own destruction. 2 Peter 3:76.
Distortion; violent pulling and twisting; perversion.
wresting: Pulling with a twist; distorting; perverting.
wrestle: To strive with arms extended, as two men, who seize each other by the collar and arms, each endeavoring to throw the other by tripping up his heels and twitching him off his center. Another, by a fall in wrestling, started the end of the clavicle from the sternum.
To struggle; to strive; to contend. We wrestle not against flesh and blood. Ephesians 6:12.
wrestler: One who wrestles; or one who is skillful in wrestling.
wretch: A miserable person; one sunk in the deepest distress; as a forlorn wretch.
Aworthless mortal; as a contemptible wretch.
A person sunk in vice; as a profligate wretch.
It is sometimes used by way of slight or ironical pity or contempt. Poor wretch was never frighted so.
It is sometimes used to express tenderness; as we say, poor thing.
wretched: Very miserable; sunk into deep affliction or distress, either from want, anxiety or grief. The wretched find no friends.
Calamitous; very afflicting; as the wretched condition of slaves in Algiers.
Worthless; paltry; very poor or mean; as a wretched poem; a wretched cabin.
Despicable; hatefully vile and contemptible. He was guilty of wretched ingratitude.
wretchedly: Most miserably; very poorly. The prisoners were wretchedly lodged.
Unhappily; as two wars wretchedly entered upon.
Meanly; despicable; as a discourse wretchedly delivered.
wretchedness: Extreme misery or unhappiness, either from want or sorrow; as the wretchedness of poor mendicants. We have, with the feeling, most the very memory of such wretchedness as our forefathers endured-- . The prodigal brought nothing to his father but his rags and wretchedness.
Meanness; despicableness; as the wretchedness of a performance.
wriggle: To move the body to and fro with short motions. Both he and his successors would often wriggle in their seats, as long as the cushion lasted.
To put into a quick reciprocating motion; to introduce by a shifting motion. Wriggling his body to recover his seat, and cast his right leg over.
wriggling: Moving the body one way and the other with quick turns.
wright: An artificer; one whose occupation is some kind of mechanical business; a workman; a manufacturer. This word is now chiefly used in compounds, as in shipwright
wring: pret. and pp. wringed and wrung. The latter is chiefly used.
To twist; to turn and strain with violence; as, to wring clothes in washing.
To squeeze; to press; to force by twisting; as, to wring water out of a wet garment.
To writhe; as, to wring the body in pain.
TO pinch. The king began to find where his shoe did wring him. If he had not been too much grieved and wrung by an uneasy and strait fortune-- .
wring-bolt: A bolt used by shipwrights, to bend and secure the planks against the timbers till they are fastened by bolts, spikes and tree-nails.
wringer: One who wrings; one that forces water out of any thing by wringing.
wring-staves: Strong bars of wood used in applying wring-bolts.
wrinkle: A small ridge or prominence, or a furrow, formed by the shrinking or contraction of any smooth substance; corrugation; a crease; as wrinkles in the face or skin.
A fold or rumple in cloth.
Roughness; unevenness. Not the least wrinkle to deform the sky.
To contract into furrows and prominences; to corrugate; as, to wrinkle the skin; to wrinkle the brow. Her wrinkled form in black and white arrayd.
To make rough or uneven. A keen north wind, blowing dry, wrinkled the face of deluge, as decayd.
wrinkling: Shrinking; contracting into furrows and ridges.
wrist: The joint by which the hand is united to the arm.
In the manege, the bridle wrist is that of the cavaliers left hand.
wristband: That band or part of a shirt sleeve which covers the wrist.
writ: That which is written. In this sense, writ is particularly applied to the Scriptures, or books of the Old Testament and New Testament; as holy writ; sacred writ.

In law, precept issued from the proper authority to the sheriff, his deputy or other subordinate officer, commanding him to perform some act, as to summon a defendant into court to answer, and the like. In England, writs are issued from some court under seal. In some of the United States, writs are issued by any single judge or justice of the peace, in the name and by the authority of the senate. In some of the United States, the writ in a civil suit, contains both the summons and the plaintiffs declaration or cause of action set forth at large, and a writ is either a summons or an attachment. Writs are original or judicial. An original writ, in England, is issued from the high court of chancery. A judicial writ is issued by order of a court upon a special occasion, during the pendency of the suit. Writs are of various kinds; as writs of assize; writs of capias; writs of distringas.
of write, is not now used.
write: To form by a pen on paper or other material, or by a graver on wood or stone; as, to write the characters called letters; to write figures. We write characters on paper with pen and ink; we write them on stone with a graving tool.

To express by forming letters and words on paper or stone; as, to write a deed; to write a bill of divorcement. The ten commandments were written with the finger of God on tables of stone. Exodus 31:18.
To impress durable. Write useful truths on the heart.
writer: One who writes or has written.
A clerk or amanuensis. Writer of the tallies, an officer of the exchequer of England; a clerk to the auditor of the receipt, who writes upon the tallies the whole of the tellers bills.
writhe: To twist; to distort. Her mouth she writhd.
To twist with violence; as, to writhe the body.
To wrest; to distort; to torture; as, to writhe words.
To twist; to be distorted; as, to writhe with agony.
writing: Forming, as characters, with a pen, style or graver. 2 .a. Used or intended for writing; as writing paper.

The act or art of forming letters and characters, on paper, wood, stone or other material, for the purpose of recording the ideas which characters and the words express, or of communicating them to others by visible signs. We hardly know which to admire most, the ingenuity or the utility of the art of writing.
Any thing written or expressed in letters; hence, any legal instrument, as a deed, a receipt, a bond, an agreement.
A book; any written composition; a pamphlet; as the writings of Addison.
writing-master: One who teaches the art of penmanship.
written: Expressed in letters. Written laws, statutes; laws enacted by the supreme power and recorded; as contradistinguished from unwritten or common law.
wrong: Literally wrung, twisted or turned from a straight line or even surface. Hence,
Not physically right; not fit or suitable; as the wrong side of a garment. You hold the book the wrong end uppermost. There may be something wrong in the construction of a watch or an edifice.

Not morally right; that deviates from the line of rectitude prescribed by God; not just or equitable; not right or proper; not legal; erroneous; as a wrong practice; wrong ideas; a wrong course of life; wrong measures; wrong inclinations and desires; a wrong application of talents; wrong judgment. Habakkuk 1:4.
Erroneous; not according to truth; as a wrong statement.

Whatever deviates from moral rectitude; any injury done to another; a trespass; a violation of right. Wrongs are private or public. Private wrongs are civil injuries, immediately affecting individuals; public wrongs are crimes and misdemeanors which affect the community. Sarai said to Abraham, my wrong be on thee. Genesis 16:5. Friend, I do thee no wrong. Matthew 20:13. The obligation to redress a wrong, is at least as binding as that of paying a debt.
wrong-doer: One who injures another, or does wrong.
wrong-doing: Evil or wicked act or action.
wronger: One who injures another. Y, the twenty fifth letter of the English Alphabet, is taken from the Greed. At the beginning of words, it is called an articulation or consonant, and with some propriety perhaps, as it brings the root of the tongue in close contact with the lower part of the palate, and nearly in the position to which the close g brings it. Hence it has happened that in a great number of words, g has been changed into y, as the Sax. Gear, into year; geornian, into yearn; gyllan, into yell; gealew, into yellow.
water-ely: An insect that is seen on the water.
watile: Properly, a twig or flexible rod; and hence, a hurdle.
wex: or i. To grow; to wax.
whe ther: Which of two alternatives, expressed by a sentence or the clause of a sentence, and followed by or. Resolve whether you will go or not; that is, you will go or not go; resolve which.
white-beaim: The white-leaf tree, a species of Crataegus.
whoredonm: Lewdness; fornication; practice of unlawful commerce with the other sex. It is applied to either sex, and to any kind of illicit commerce. WHOREDOM.
In Scripture, idolatry; the desertion of the worship of the true God, for the worship of idols. WHOREDOM.
wigwanm: An Indian cabin or hut, so called in America. It is sometimes written weekwam.
winter-rig: To fallow or till in winter.
withernaim: In withernam, in law, a second or reciprocal distress, in lieu of a first distress which has been eloigned; reprisal.
wolfs-bane: The winter aconite, or Helleborus hyemalis.
wormwood-fely: A small black fly, found on the stalks of wormwood.
wormy: Containing a worm; abounding with worms.
wrongful: Injurious unjust; as a wrongful taking of property; wrongful dealing.
wrongfully: Unjustly; in a manner contrary to the moral law or to justice; as, to accuse one wrongfully; to suffer wrongfully.
wronghead: Wrong in opinion or principle; having a perverse understanding; perverse.
wrongheaded: Wrong in opinion or principle; having a perverse understanding; perverse.
wrongly: In a wrong manner; unjustly; amiss. He judges wrongly of my motives.
wrote: of write. He wrote a letter yesterday. Herodotus wrote his history more than two thousand years ago.
wroth: Rauth. Very angry; much exasperated. Cain was very wroth, and his countenance fell. Genesis 4:5. I was wroth with my people. Isaiah 47:6.
wrought: Worked; formed by work or labor; as wrought iron.
Effected; performed. She hath wrought a good work upon me. Matthew 26:10.
Effected; produced. He wrought the public safety. A great change was wrought in his mind. This wrought the greatest confusion int he unbelieving Jews.
Used in labor. The elders of that city shall take a heifer that hath not been wrought with. Deuteronomy 21:3.
Worked; driven; as infection wrought out of the body.
wry: Twisted; turned to one side; distorted; as a wry neck; a wry mouth.
Deviating from the right direction; as wry words.
Wrested; perverted; as, to put a wry sense on an authors words.
wryneck: A twisted or distorted neck; a deformity in which the neck is drawn to one side, and at the same time somewhat forwards./ .
A disease of the spasmodic kind in sheep, in which the head is drawn to one side.
In ornithology, a bird resembling the woodpeckers, the Yunx torquilla; so called form the singular manner in which when surprised, it tuns its head over its shoulders.
wryness: The state of being wry or distorted.
wych-elm: A variety of the elm, or a peculiar species, (Ulmus glabra.) X X, the twenty fourth letter of the English Alphabet, is borrowed from the Greek. In the middle and at the end of words, it has the sound of ks, as in wax, laxAt the beginning of a word, it has precisely the sound of z. It is used as an initial, in a few words borrowed from the Greek. As a numeral, X stands for ten. It represents one V, which stands for five, placed on the top of another. When laid horizontally it stands for a thousand, and with a dash over it, it stands for ten thousand. As an abbreviation, X. Stands for Christ, and in Xn. Christian; Xm. Christmas. X.
A variety of the elm, or a peculiar species, (Ulmus glabra.) the eyes, without swelling or a discharge of humors.
